simple: ABCG1 is a cellular pump that moves cholesterol and related fatty molecules out of cells, using energy from the cell's fuel molecule ATP. This helps keep the right balance of fats inside cells, which is especially important in immune cells called macrophages. By exporting harmful forms of cholesterol, it also helps protect cells from being damaged or killed.
technical: ABCG1 is an ATP-binding cassette transporter that catalyzes the ATP-dependent efflux of phospholipids such as sphingomyelin and of cholesterol and its oxygenated derivatives, including 7beta-hydroxycholesterol, with lipid efflux being albumin-dependent. It is an active component of the macrophage lipid export complex and contributes to cellular lipid homeostasis, a role that is not limited to macrophages. By exporting cytotoxic 7beta-hydroxycholesterol, it prevents cell death, and it may also participate in intracellular lipid transport. No specific Mendelian disease is established for this gene in the provided data.

simple: ADAMTS1 is an enzyme that acts like molecular scissors in the space between cells, trimming large structural proteins that give tissues such as cartilage their shape and resilience. It also helps limit the growth of new blood vessels and may play a role in the release of eggs from the ovary. Through these activities it contributes to tissue remodeling and normal reproductive function.
technical: ADAMTS1 is a secreted, extracellular-matrix metalloproteinase of the ADAMTS family that cleaves the cartilage proteoglycan aggrecan within its chondroitin sulfate attachment domain and also cleaves COMP, contributing to proteoglycan turnover. It additionally exhibits anti-angiogenic activity and may be critical for follicular rupture during ovulation. No specific disease association is provided in the source data.

simple: ADAMTS5 is an enzyme that trims large cushioning proteins found in cartilage and other connective tissues, helping shape and maintain these tissues. It is important during development of limbs, the heart, and muscle, and it helps immune cells move around the body. By breaking down certain matrix proteins, it influences how tissues are built, repaired, and remodeled.
technical: ADAMTS5 is a secreted extracellular-matrix metalloproteinase that degrades hyalectan-family chondroitin sulfate proteoglycans, including aggrecan (ACAN), versican (VCAN), brevican (BCAN) and neurocan (NCAN), cleaving at Glu-Xaa recognition motifs. Through versican remodeling in the pericellular matrix it facilitates myoblast contact and fusion required for skeletal muscle development and regeneration, and it contributes to limb and cardiac morphogenesis and to brown/white adipose tissue biology. It also promotes T-lymphocyte egress from draining lymph nodes following viral infection. No specific disease association is provided in the source data.

simple: ADARB1 is an enzyme that chemically edits RNA, the messenger copies of our genes, by changing one of its building blocks. These small edits can subtly alter the proteins the cell makes, which is especially important for the proper function of brain signaling molecules. When this enzyme is faulty, children can develop a severe brain disorder with developmental delay, intellectual disability, and difficult-to-control seizures.
technical: ADARB1 (also known as ADAR2) is an adenosine deaminase that catalyzes hydrolytic deamination of adenosine to inosine within double-stranded RNA (A-to-I editing), thereby recoding transcripts, altering splice-site recognition, and influencing RNA stability and structure-dependent processes. Well-characterized neuronal substrates include GRIA2 (efficient editing at the Q/R and R/G sites), GRIK2, the serotonin receptor HTR2C, GABRA3, KCNA1 and BLCAP, where site-specific editing produces amino acid substitutions that modify protein function. Biallelic loss-of-function variants cause an autosomal recessive neurodevelopmental disorder with global developmental delay, axial hypotonia, impaired intellectual development with absent language, and early-onset intractable seizures, often with microcephaly and cortical visual impairment.

simple: AGPAT3 is an enzyme that helps build the fatty molecules cells use to make membranes and store energy. It adds a fatty acid to a lipid precursor, a key step in producing the building blocks of cell membranes. This activity supports normal fat metabolism in the body.
technical: AGPAT3 (1-acyl-sn-glycerol-3-phosphate acyltransferase gamma) converts lysophosphatidic acid (LPA) to phosphatidic acid (PA) by transferring an acyl group to the sn-2 position of the glycerol backbone, acting on LPA species bearing C16:0-C20:4 acyl chains and showing a preference for arachidonoyl-CoA as the acyl donor. It can also acylate lysophosphatidylcholine, lysophosphatidylinositol and lysophosphatidylserine, and exhibits modest lysophosphatidylinositol acyltransferase activity, contributing to glycerophospholipid biosynthesis. No specific disease association is provided in the source data.

simple: AIRE is a master switch in the thymus, the organ where immune cells learn the difference between the body's own tissues and foreign invaders. It turns on thousands of genes normally active only in distant organs, so that developing immune cells can be tested against them and dangerous self-reactive cells can be eliminated. When AIRE is defective, the immune system attacks the body's own glands and tissues, causing a rare inherited autoimmune disease.
technical: AIRE is a transcriptional regulator expressed mainly in medullary thymic epithelial cells (mTECs) that drives promiscuous expression of thousands of tissue-restricted self-antigens presented on MHC-I and MHC-II to developing thymocytes, thereby promoting central tolerance. It binds chromatin and acts as a sensor of histone H3 modifications, interacting selectively with H3 unmethylated at Lys4, and also shapes mTEC differentiation, thymic dendritic-cell accumulation, and regulatory T-cell development via chemokine regulation (e.g., XCL1, CCR4/CCR7 ligands). Loss-of-function mutations cause autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type 1 (APECED/APS1), characterized by chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis, hypoparathyroidism and Addison disease, frequently with additional autoimmune manifestations such as type 1 diabetes, vitiligo, alopecia, hepatitis and pernicious anemia.

simple: APP is a protein on the surface of nerve cells that helps neurons grow, connect, and form the junctions through which they communicate. When it is cut into smaller pieces, it produces amyloid-beta fragments that can clump together into the sticky plaques found in the brains of people with Alzheimer's disease. Mutations in APP can cause inherited forms of Alzheimer's disease and a condition in which amyloid builds up in brain blood vessels, leading to strokes and bleeding.
technical: APP is a single-pass transmembrane cell-surface protein that acts as a receptor influencing neurite outgrowth, neuronal adhesion, synaptogenesis and axonal transport (serving as a kinesin-I membrane receptor), and it participates in copper homeostasis and transcriptional regulation through protein-protein interactions. Sequential proteolysis by beta- and gamma-secretases generates amyloid-beta peptides (notably Abeta40 and Abeta42), which are lipophilic metal chelators that aggregate into extracellular plaques, while C-terminal fragments and caspase-cleaved products such as C31 promote neuronal apoptosis. Mutations cause autosomal dominant Alzheimer disease, characterized by progressive dementia with neurofibrillary tangles and amyloid plaques, and hereditary cerebral amyloid angiopathy with recurrent cerebral/cerebellar hemorrhage, strokes and progressive mental deterioration.

simple: ATP5PF is one piece of the cell's main power plant, an enzyme machine in mitochondria that produces ATP, the molecule cells use as energy currency. It belongs to the supporting stalk that holds the machine steady while it spins to make energy. Without these parts working together, cells cannot generate the fuel they need.
technical: ATP5PF is subunit F6 of the peripheral (stator) stalk of the mitochondrial F1Fo-ATP synthase (Complex V), located in the inner mitochondrial membrane, which synthesizes ATP from ADP using the proton-motive force generated by the respiratory chain. As part of the peripheral stalk it helps hold the catalytic alpha3beta3 head and the membrane subunit a/ATP6 static relative to the rotating central stalk during the rotary catalytic cycle. No specific disease association is provided in the source data.

simple: ATP5PO is part of the cell's main energy-producing machine in mitochondria, which makes ATP, the fuel that powers nearly all cellular activities. It forms part of the structural stalk that connects and stabilizes the moving and stationary parts of this machine. When it is defective, infants can develop a severe whole-body disorder affecting the brain, muscles, and heart.
technical: ATP5PO is the OSCP subunit of the peripheral stalk of the mitochondrial F1Fo-ATP synthase (Complex V) in the inner mitochondrial membrane, which produces ATP from ADP coupled to proton translocation driven by the respiratory chain. As a stator component it helps couple proton flow to ATP synthesis by holding the catalytic F1 head and subunit a static relative to the rotating central stalk. Biallelic defects cause an autosomal recessive, severe infantile-onset mitochondrial disorder with hypotonia, developmental delay, progressive epileptic encephalopathy, cerebral atrophy with white matter abnormalities, and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy in some patients, often with death in infancy or early childhood.

simple: B3GALT5 is an enzyme that attaches a specific sugar onto other sugar chains found on proteins and fats at the cell surface. These sugar decorations help define how cells are recognized and how they interact with their surroundings. This kind of sugar-building is part of the normal chemistry that gives cell-surface molecules their identity.
technical: B3GALT5 (beta-1,3-galactosyltransferase 5) catalyzes transfer of galactose to GlcNAc-based acceptors, with a preference for the core 3 O-linked glycan GlcNAc(beta1,3)GalNAc structure, and can also use the glycolipid Lc3Cer as an efficient acceptor. It thereby contributes to the biosynthesis of type 1 chain glycans on glycoproteins and glycolipids. No specific disease association is provided in the source data.

simple: BACE2 is an enzyme that cuts other proteins at precise spots, including the amyloid precursor protein linked to Alzheimer's disease. It also helps process proteins involved in making pigment (giving color to skin and eyes) and in pancreatic cells that regulate blood sugar. Through these cutting actions it shapes how several important proteins mature and function.
technical: BACE2 (beta-secretase 2) is a transmembrane aspartyl protease that proteolytically processes the amyloid precursor protein (APP), generating beta-cleaved soluble APP and a C-terminal fragment subsequently handled by gamma-secretase. It also cleaves PMEL within the M-beta fragment, a prerequisite step for melanosomal amyloid fibril/sheet formation during melanosome biogenesis, and processes CLTRN (collectrin) in pancreatic beta cells. No specific disease association is provided in the source data.

simple: BACH1 is a protein that controls which genes are switched on or off, acting as either a brake or an accelerator depending on the situation. It is especially important in the cell's response to oxidative stress, the chemical damage caused by reactive molecules. By tuning these protective genes, it helps regulate how cells defend themselves.
technical: BACH1 is a BTB/CNC bZIP transcription factor that binds NF-E2/MARE DNA sites and acts as either a repressor or activator depending on context, coordinating transcription together with small Maf proteins such as MAFK and MAF. In partnership with MAF it represses genes controlled by the NFE2L2 (NRF2) antioxidant/oxidative-stress pathway, thereby modulating the cellular response to oxidative stress. No specific disease association is provided in the source data.

simple: BAGE is a poorly understood protein whose normal job in the body is not known. It was originally identified because it is recognized by a patient's own immune cells on a melanoma (a type of skin cancer), marking it as a tumor antigen. Because of this, it is of interest mainly in the context of cancer recognition by the immune system, though its biological function remains unclear.
technical: BAGE (B melanoma antigen 1) has no established molecular function; it was identified as a tumor antigen recognized on a melanoma by autologous cytolytic T-lymphocytes. It belongs to the cancer/testis antigen category of interest in tumor immunology, but its physiological role is uncharacterized. No specific disease association is provided in the source data beyond its identification as a melanoma antigen.

simple: BAGE3 is a poorly characterized protein whose normal biological role is unknown. It belongs to a family of genes thought to produce tumor antigens, meaning proteins that can be recognized in cancer cells. Beyond this candidate role in cancer, little is firmly established about what it does.
technical: BAGE3 (B melanoma antigen 3) is a member of the BAGE family with no established molecular function; it is a candidate tumor-antigen-encoding gene. Its physiological role and localization are uncharacterized. No specific disease association is provided in the source data.

simple: BAGE4 is a poorly characterized protein whose normal biological role is unknown. It is part of a gene family considered candidate sources of tumor antigens, proteins that may be recognized in cancer cells. Beyond this possible cancer-related role, little is firmly established about its function.
technical: BAGE4 (B melanoma antigen 4) is a member of the BAGE family with no established molecular function; it is a candidate tumor-antigen-encoding gene. Its physiological role and subcellular localization are uncharacterized. No specific disease association is provided in the source data.

simple: BRWD1 is a protein thought to help control which genes are active by influencing how DNA is packaged in the cell. It is involved in maintaining proper cell shape and the internal scaffolding of cells. When it is defective, it can cause a disorder affecting the tiny hair-like structures (cilia) that clear the airways and power sperm tails, leading to recurrent respiratory infections and male infertility.
technical: BRWD1 (bromodomain and WD repeat-containing protein 1) is a putative transcriptional activator implicated in chromatin remodeling and required for regulation of cell morphology and cytoskeletal organization. Biallelic loss-of-function variants cause primary ciliary dyskinesia type 51 (CILD51), an autosomal recessive disorder with motile cilia abnormalities manifesting as recurrent upper and lower respiratory infections and bronchiectasis, and male infertility due to multiple morphologic abnormalities of the sperm flagella with severely reduced motility; some affected individuals show dextrocardia and/or situs inversus.

simple: BTG3 is a protein that helps put the brakes on cell division. When present in excess, it slows cells from moving out of their resting state into the phase where they copy their DNA to divide. This suggests it plays a role in keeping cell growth in check.
technical: BTG3 is a member of the BTG/TOB antiproliferative protein family; its overexpression impairs serum-induced cell cycle progression from G0/G1 to S phase, consistent with a growth-suppressive role. Beyond this antiproliferative effect, detailed molecular mechanisms are not specified in the provided data. No specific disease association is provided in the source data.

simple: C21orf140 is an uncharacterized protein, meaning scientists have identified the gene on chromosome 21 but do not yet know what its protein does. Very little is firmly established about its role in the body. Further research would be needed to determine its function and any importance to health.
technical: C21orf140 is an uncharacterized open reading frame on chromosome 21 with no established molecular function, localization, pathway, or disease association in the provided data. Although a protein-level accession (B9A014) exists, the source provides no functional annotation, so any specific functional claim would be unfounded and the protein product should be regarded as poorly characterized.

simple: This is a poorly understood protein found on chromosome 21, and scientists have not yet pinned down what it actually does in the body. Its name simply reflects where its gene sits on the chromosome rather than any known job. For now, little is firmly established about its role in health or disease.
technical: C21orf58 is an uncharacterized protein encoded on chromosome 21 for which no molecular function, subcellular localization, or pathway involvement has been firmly established. The UniProt entry (P58505) provides no functional annotation, so any role must be considered unknown. There are no well-established disease associations reported for this protein.

simple: This protein is thought to help cells detect and dispose of worn-out energy factories called mitochondria, working alongside a partner protein. By helping package and expel these aging components, it may contribute to keeping cells healthy. Its exact role is still being investigated.
technical: C21orf62 (exosomal polycystin-1-interacting protein) is proposed to act together with PKD1 (polycystin-1) in the detection, sequestration, and exocytosis of senescent mitochondria. This suggests a role in mitochondrial quality control and possibly extracellular vesicle-mediated disposal pathways. No well-established disease associations have been reported for this protein.

simple: This protein helps the developing brain by supporting the growth and maturation of nerve cells in the cortex, the brain's outer layer. It encourages neurons to form the small connection points they use to communicate with each other. This makes it relevant to how the brain wires itself during development.
technical: C21orf91 (protein EURL homolog) functions in cortical progenitor cell proliferation and differentiation, and promotes dendritic spine development in post-migratory cortical projection neurons by modulating the beta-catenin signaling pathway. Its activity links it to neurodevelopmental processes in the cerebral cortex. No well-established disease associations are currently reported.

simple: This protein carries a structural module (a C2 domain) that in many proteins helps bind to cell membranes, often in a way that responds to calcium. However, its specific job in the cell has not been clearly established. For now, much about its function remains uncertain.
technical: C2CD2 (C2 domain-containing protein 2) is named for its C2 domain, a module commonly associated with calcium-dependent membrane targeting and lipid binding, but the UniProt entry (Q9Y426) provides no firm functional annotation. Its precise molecular function, subcellular localization, and pathway involvement remain to be established. No well-established disease associations have been reported.

simple: This enzyme acts as a general-purpose detoxifier, chemically modifying a wide range of compounds so the body can process them, using a helper molecule derived from vitamin precursors. It handles many natural substances and foreign chemicals, and notably converts certain chemotherapy drugs into forms that can harm the heart. It also plays a part in handling stress hormones.
technical: CBR1 (carbonyl reductase [NADPH] 1) is a cytosolic NADPH-dependent reductase with broad substrate specificity, reducing carbonyl compounds including quinones, prostaglandins, menadione, and numerous xenobiotics. Clinically notable, it reduces the anthracyclines doxorubicin and daunorubicin to the cardiotoxic alcohol metabolites doxorubicinol and daunorubicinol, and it also participates in glucocorticoid metabolism (e.g., conversion of cortisol/corticosterone to 20beta-dihydro metabolites) and can reduce S-nitrosoglutathione. Its glutathione-binding capacity gives it higher affinity for glutathione-conjugated substrates. No specific Mendelian disease is established, though its role in anthracycline cardiotoxicity is well recognized.

simple: This enzyme converts certain carbonyl-containing compounds into alcohols, using a helper molecule, and seems especially active on a class of reactive chemicals called quinones. By doing so it may help protect cells from the damage some of these compounds can cause. Few natural targets of this enzyme are known so far.
technical: CBR3 (carbonyl reductase [NADPH] 3) is a cytosolic enzyme catalyzing NADPH-dependent reduction of carbonyl compounds to their corresponding alcohols, with activity toward several ortho-quinones (but not para-quinones), non-quinone compounds such as isatin, and the anticancer drug oracin. Its preferred substrate is 1,2-naphthoquinone, suggesting a possible protective role against the cytotoxicity of exogenous quinones. Aside from isatin, no endogenous substrate has been identified, and no well-established disease associations are reported.

simple: This enzyme performs the first step in a pathway that lets the body get rid of an amino acid building block and clear away homocysteine, a substance that is toxic when it builds up. It also helps produce hydrogen sulfide, a signaling gas that can protect nerve cells. When this enzyme is faulty, homocysteine accumulates and causes a serious inherited condition.
technical: CBS (cystathionine beta-synthase) is a hydro-lyase catalyzing the first and committed step of the transsulfuration pathway, condensing L-serine and L-homocysteine into L-cystathionine, the precursor of L-cysteine, thereby clearing toxic L-homocysteine and excess L-methionine. It also contributes to hydrogen sulfide production, a gasotransmitter with cytoprotective effects on neurons. Loss-of-function mutations cause classic homocystinuria, characterized by myopia, ectopia lentis, intellectual disability, Marfan-like skeletal anomalies, and thromboembolic events, with biochemical elevation of urinary homocystine and methionine.

simple: This is a predicted gene whose protein product has not been firmly characterized, and its name suggests it is a close relative of the well-studied CBS (cystathionine beta-synthase) gene. Because of that similarity, it is presumed to resemble that enzyme family, but specific details about what it does have not been established. Little can be said with confidence about its role.
technical: CBSL is a predicted, poorly characterized locus with no resolved UniProt accession and no firm functional annotation. The symbol indicates a presumed paralog of CBS (cystathionine beta-synthase), so its product is predicted to resemble the cystathionine beta-synthase family, but no specific molecular function, localization, or pathway role should be asserted. No well-established disease associations have been documented for this gene.

simple: This protein is part of a cellular machine that acts like a folding station, helping freshly made proteins fold into their correct shapes so they can work properly. Among the proteins it helps fold are the building blocks of the cell's internal scaffolding. This folding help is essential for normal cell function.
technical: CCT8 (T-complex protein 1 subunit theta) is a subunit of the chaperonin-containing TCP-1 complex (TRiC/CCT), a cytosolic molecular chaperone that assists ATP-dependent folding of substrates including actin, tubulin, and other proteins. Through the TRiC complex it contributes to folding of WRAP53/TCAB1 (regulating telomere maintenance) and to assembly of the BBSome, a complex involved in ciliogenesis and vesicle transport to cilia. No well-established disease associations are reported in the provided annotation.

simple: This protein is needed for the tiny hair-like structures called cilia to beat properly, which is important for clearing the airways and for setting up the body's left-right layout during early development. When it is defective, cilia do not move correctly, leading to chronic airway infections and sometimes organs being mirror-imaged. This underlies a condition known as primary ciliary dyskinesia.
technical: CFAP298 (cilia- and flagella-associated protein 298) is required for motile cilium function, likely acting in outer dynein arm assembly and important for the initiation of ciliary motility, and it is also implicated in correct apical positioning of the cilium (planar cell polarity) and possible suppression of canonical Wnt signaling. Defects cause primary ciliary dyskinesia, with recurrent respiratory infections, chronic inflammation, and bronchiectasis; randomization of left-right asymmetry can produce situs inversus (Kartagener syndrome when combined with situs inversus). Its function is tied to the axoneme of motile cilia and to embryonic nodal monocilia.

simple: This entry refers to a read-through combination of two neighboring genes on chromosome 21, and the protein it might produce has not been firmly characterized. Because it is a predicted fusion-type transcript, little is established about whether it makes a functional protein or what that protein would do. Caution is warranted in interpreting its role.
technical: CFAP298-TCP10L denotes a predicted read-through locus joining the neighboring CFAP298 and TCP10L genes, with no resolved UniProt accession and no firm functional annotation. The molecular function, localization, and pathway involvement of any encoded product are not established, and read-through transcripts may or may not yield a stable functional protein. No well-established disease associations are documented for this entry specifically.

simple: This protein helps build and maintain cilia, the small projections on cells, and also helps organize a cell's internal shape and repair damaged DNA. When it is faulty, it can cause eye diseases that progressively worsen vision and a skeletal growth disorder affecting the spine and long bones. These conditions can appear together in affected individuals.
technical: CFAP410 (cilia- and flagella-associated protein 410) functions in cilia formation/maintenance, regulation of cell morphology and cytoskeletal organization, and DNA damage repair. Pathogenic variants are associated with a progressive retinal dystrophy (with retinal pigment epithelium mottling, vessel narrowing, and features of cone-rod dystrophy or pigmentary retinal degeneration) and with axial spondylometaphyseal dysplasia (SMDAX), a short-stature disorder featuring metaphyseal changes of truncal-juxtatruncal bones, rhizomelic then short-trunk stature, thoracic hypoplasia, and characteristic radiological findings. The dual ocular and skeletal phenotype reflects its roles in both ciliary and cytoskeletal/genome-maintenance processes.

simple: This protein is part of a cellular team that packages freshly copied DNA by loading it onto spool-like histone proteins, which is a key step every time a cell divides or repairs its DNA. By doing this it helps organize the genome into its proper compact form. This packaging is fundamental to keeping genetic information stable.
technical: CHAF1B (chromatin assembly factor 1 subunit B) is a component of the CAF-1 histone chaperone complex, which performs the first step of nucleosome assembly during DNA replication and repair by depositing newly synthesized histones H3 and H4 onto nascent DNA. Subsequent binding of H2A/H2B completes the histone octamer to form nucleosomes. No well-established disease associations are provided in the source annotation.

simple: This protein appears to help the nervous system develop, particularly by supporting the growth and guidance of nerve fibers, including those of motor nerves that control muscles. By helping neurons extend and find their targets, it may contribute to proper wiring of the nervous system. Its precise role is still being explored.
technical: CHODL (chondrolectin) is implicated in nervous system development, potentially in neurite outgrowth and elongation, and may participate in motor axon growth and guidance. These proposed roles point to functions in neuronal connectivity, though the molecular details remain incompletely defined. No well-established disease associations are reported in the provided annotation.

simple: This protein helps form tight seals between neighboring cells, controlling what can pass through the spaces between them. It plays an important role in the inner ear, and when it is defective it leads to a form of inherited hearing loss. This makes it essential for normal hearing.
technical: CLDN14 (claudin-14) is a tight junction protein that contributes to sealing the intercellular space via calcium-independent cell-adhesion activity, thereby regulating paracellular permeability. Loss-of-function mutations cause a form of non-syndromic sensorineural hearing loss, reflecting its critical role at tight junctions in the inner ear. Sensorineural deafness in this context arises from damage to inner ear neural receptors or associated pathways.

simple: This protein forms channels in the seals between cells that selectively let through negatively charged particles, such as chloride and bicarbonate, while blocking larger molecules. In the kidney it may help reclaim these charged substances from the fluid being filtered. It does not change how water moves across the barrier.
technical: CLDN17 (claudin-17) is a channel-forming tight junction protein conferring anion selectivity, permitting passage of anions such as chloride and bicarbonate and of solutes smaller than ~9 Angstrom, without affecting water permeability. In the renal proximal tubule it is proposed to mediate paracellular reabsorption of filtered anions. No well-established disease associations are reported in the provided annotation.

simple: This protein works with other related proteins to fine-tune the sealing barriers between cells, helping control what passes through the gaps between them. In the kidney it may team up with a partner to form channels that allow chloride to pass, which matters for fluid and salt balance. On its own it cannot build these cell-to-cell seals.
technical: CLDN8 (claudin-8) associates with other claudins to regulate the structure and function of tight junction strands and cannot form strands on its own. It may co-assemble with CLDN4 to create tight junction strands containing anion-selective channels that confer paracellular chloride permeability in renal collecting ducts. No well-established disease associations are reported in the provided annotation.

simple: This protein has a dual nature: in its dissolved form it helps manage the cell's chemistry by participating in protective antioxidant-style reactions, and it can also insert into membranes to form channels that let chloride pass depending on the electrical state of the membrane. It may be especially important in cells that secrete water. Its full role is still being studied.
technical: CLIC6 (chloride intracellular channel protein 6) exists in a soluble form that catalyzes glutaredoxin-like thiol-disulfide exchange reactions using reduced glutathione as electron donor, and can also insert into membranes to form voltage-dependent, chloride-selective channels that open upon depolarization at positive voltages and close at negative voltages. It is proposed to be important in water-secreting cells, possibly through regulation of chloride transport. No well-established disease associations are reported in the provided annotation.

simple: This protein is part of collagen XVIII, a structural building block found in the connective tissue scaffolding around cells, and it is especially important for the proper development of the eye and the brain. It also acts as a brake on the growth of new blood vessels, helping keep tissues from becoming over-supplied with vessels. Faults in this protein cause an inherited disorder marked by severe eye problems such as high myopia, cataracts, lens dislocation, and retinal detachment, sometimes with skull defects, and it is also linked to a form of glaucoma.
technical: COL18A1 encodes the alpha-1 chain of collagen XVIII, a basement-membrane-associated collagen that influences neural tube closure and retinal architecture and can regulate extracellular-matrix-dependent cell motility and morphogenesis through homotrimerization and MAPK signaling. It is a potent inhibitor of endothelial cell proliferation and angiogenesis, acting in part by binding heparan sulfate proteoglycans and by blocking VEGFA engagement of its receptor KDR/VEGFR2, and it modulates endothelial migration via integrins such as ITGA5:ITGB1. Loss-of-function variants cause Knobloch syndrome, a developmental disorder featuring high myopia, cataracts, dislocated lens, vitreoretinal degeneration, retinal detachment, and occipital skull defects. Variants are also associated with an autosomal dominant form of primary angle-closure glaucoma characterized by elevated intraocular pressure.

simple: This protein is one of the chains that make up collagen VI, a component of the supportive mesh that surrounds and anchors cells, particularly in muscle. By acting as a cell-binding protein, it helps connect cells to their surrounding tissue. When it is defective, it causes inherited muscle diseases such as Bethlem myopathy and Ullrich congenital muscular dystrophy, which lead to muscle weakness, joint stiffness or contractures, and unusually flexible joints.
technical: COL6A1 encodes the alpha-1 chain of collagen VI, an extracellular matrix protein that functions as a cell-binding protein and contributes to the microfibrillar network linking cells to the surrounding matrix in skeletal muscle and other tissues. Pathogenic variants cause a spectrum of collagen VI-related myopathies. These include Bethlem myopathy, a slowly progressive muscular dystrophy with proximal and extensor muscle weakness and characteristic long finger flexion contractures, and the more severe Ullrich congenital muscular dystrophy, marked by generalized weakness, proximal joint contractures, and striking distal joint hypermobility. Inheritance of these conditions can be autosomal dominant or autosomal recessive.

simple: This protein is another chain of collagen VI, working alongside its partner chains to form part of the supportive mesh around cells and to help cells attach to their surrounding tissue, especially in muscle. When it does not work properly, it causes inherited muscle disorders such as Bethlem myopathy and Ullrich congenital muscular dystrophy, which bring muscle weakness, joint contractures, and loose joints. Certain defects are also linked to a condition with chronic muscle inflammation and stiff, woody-feeling muscles.
technical: COL6A2 encodes the alpha-2 chain of collagen VI, an extracellular matrix protein acting as a cell-binding protein within the collagen VI microfibrillar network that connects cells to the surrounding matrix. Pathogenic variants cause collagen VI-related myopathies, including Bethlem myopathy, a slowly progressive muscular dystrophy with predominantly proximal and extensor muscle weakness and characteristic finger flexion contractures, and Ullrich congenital muscular dystrophy, featuring generalized weakness, proximal joint contractures, and distal joint hypermobility; both can be inherited in an autosomal dominant or recessive manner. Variants are additionally associated with a myosclerosis-type condition characterized by chronic inflammation of skeletal muscle, interstitial connective tissue hyperplasia, firm 'woody' muscles, and joint movement restriction due to contractures.

simple: This protein is a major component of the lens of the eye, where it helps keep the lens clear and properly focuses light. It also acts as a protective chaperone, preventing other proteins from clumping together when the cell is under stress. When it is faulty, it causes cataracts, a clouding of the lens that blurs vision and can lead to blindness, sometimes alongside an abnormally small cornea.
technical: CRYAA encodes alpha-crystallin A chain, a small heat-shock-family lens protein that contributes to the transparency and refractive index of the crystalline lens. In its reduced (oxidized, disulfide-free) form it functions as a molecular chaperone, preventing aggregation of diverse proteins under stress, and it is required for correct assembly of lens intermediate filaments as part of a complex with BFSP1 and BFSP2. Pathogenic variants cause autosomal cataract (CTRCT9), an opacification of the lens with variable morphology that frequently impairs vision, in some cases occurring together with microcornea (corneal diameter under 10 mm) in an otherwise normal eye.

simple: This is a predicted or poorly characterized gene, and little is firmly established about the protein it may produce. Its name suggests a possible relationship to the alpha-crystallin A lens protein family, but this should be treated cautiously and no specific function has been confirmed. More research is needed before reliable conclusions can be drawn about what, if anything, this protein does.
technical: CRYAA2 is an unresolved locus with no reviewed accession and no curated functional annotation, so essentially nothing is firmly established about its protein product. Based solely on its symbol, it is presumed to be a paralog or duplicate related to the alpha-crystallin A (CRYAA) family, but this relationship and any inferred crystallin or small-heat-shock-protein chaperone activity remain predicted and unverified. No subcellular localization, pathway involvement, or disease association can be reliably asserted from the available grounding data.

simple: Despite a name historically linked to crystallins, this protein actually works inside cells as part of a machine that helps move genetic messenger molecules (mRNAs) to the right places. It is a component of a complex that attaches these messages to small cellular compartments called early endosomes so they can be transported and used where needed. This kind of internal cargo-sorting helps keep cells organized and functioning properly.
technical: CRYZL1 encodes a subunit of the FERRY complex (Five-subunit Endosomal Rab5 and RNA/ribosome intermediarY), which functions as a RAB5A effector in endosomal mRNA transport. The complex directly binds mRNAs and recruits both mRNAs and ribosomes to early endosomes, mediating the localization and distribution of specific transcripts via RAB5A-positive early endosomes. No well-established disease association is reported in the provided data.

simple: This protein acts as a built-in safety switch inside cells, blocking certain protein-cutting enzymes so they do not damage the cell's own components. By keeping these enzymes in check, it helps protect cells, particularly in the brain. When it is faulty, it causes a serious inherited form of epilepsy that begins in childhood with involuntary muscle jerks and seizures and gradually worsens with declining mental function.
technical: CSTB encodes cystatin-B, an intracellular thiol (cysteine) proteinase inhibitor that acts as a tightly binding, reversible inhibitor of lysosomal cathepsins L, H, and B. By restraining these proteases, it helps protect cells against unregulated proteolysis. Loss-of-function variants cause Unverricht-Lundborg disease (progressive myoclonic epilepsy type 1, EPM1), an autosomal recessive disorder with onset between 6 and 13 years featuring severe stimulus-sensitive myoclonus, tonic-clonic seizures, and progressive neurodegeneration with cognitive decline.

simple: This protein sits at the junctions between cells in linings such as those of the gut and airways, where it acts like molecular glue that holds neighboring cells tightly together and helps immune cells pass through to repair and maintain tissue. It is also the doorway that certain viruses exploit to enter cells, including some Coxsackieviruses and adenoviruses. Its dual roles in tissue integrity, immune function, and viral entry make it important in both health and infection.
technical: CXADR encodes the coxsackievirus and adenovirus receptor, a component of the epithelial apical junction complex that acts as a homophilic cell adhesion molecule essential for tight junction integrity. It mediates transepithelial leukocyte migration through interactions with JAML and triggers JAML-dependent activation, proliferation, and cytokine production in gamma-delta T-cells via PI3-kinase and MAP kinase signaling, supporting epithelial tissue repair and homeostasis. It also serves as the cell-surface receptor exploited by adenovirus type C and Coxsackievirus B1-B6 for entry. No well-established inherited disease association is reported in the provided data.

simple: This protein has not been well studied, and its specific job in the body is not yet clearly established. It is named for being rich in two particular building blocks, the amino acids cysteine and tyrosine, but a defined function has not been confirmed. Because little firm information is available, any role it plays remains to be determined by further research.
technical: CYYR1 encodes cysteine and tyrosine-rich protein 1, a protein for which no curated molecular function is provided in the available data. Beyond the compositional feature implied by its name, its molecular activity, subcellular localization, and pathway involvement are not firmly established. No disease association is reported, and any functional claims would be speculative; further characterization is needed.

simple: This protein appears to help cells produce a key building-block molecule used to chemically tag other proteins, and through this it supports the proper shaping of connections between brain cells, which is important for how nerve cells communicate. It can also sit on the cell surface and respond to a signaling protein, helping certain cells survive, including cells lining blood vessels and heart muscle cells. These roles connect it to both brain function and the health of the heart and circulatory system.
technical: DIP2A encodes disco-interacting protein 2 homolog A, which has been reported to catalyze de novo synthesis of acetyl-CoA and to promote acetylation of cortactin (CTTN), thereby contributing to correct dendritic spine morphology and synaptic transmission. It also binds the secreted protein FSTL1 and may act as a cell-surface receptor for FSTL1, contributing to AKT activation and FSTL1-induced survival and function of endothelial cells and cardiac myocytes. No well-established disease association is reported in the provided data.

simple: This protein is thought to help other proteins fold into their correct shapes or to act as a helper that keeps them from misfolding. Proteins like this, often called chaperones, are important for keeping cells healthy. Its exact role is not yet well defined, so much about what it does remains to be confirmed.
technical: DNAJC28 encodes a member of the DnaJ (Hsp40) subfamily C of co-chaperones, which is predicted to function in protein folding or as a molecular chaperone, typically by regulating Hsp70-family activity. Its specific substrates, subcellular localization, and pathway context are not firmly established in the available data. No disease association is reported.

simple: This protein cannot add chemical methyl tags to DNA itself, but it acts as a regulator that switches the DNA-tagging enzymes on or off depending on the situation, helping decide which genes are marked and silenced. It is especially important in stem cells and in the cells that give rise to eggs and sperm, where it helps set up the correct gene-marking patterns, including those needed for genomic imprinting and for keeping mobile DNA elements in check. In this way it helps control which genes are active during early development.
technical: DNMT3L encodes a catalytically inactive regulatory cofactor of the de novo DNA methyltransferases DNMT3A and DNMT3B. It binds their catalytic domains to stimulate activity by accelerating DNA and S-adenosyl-L-methionine binding, recognizes unmethylated histone H3K4 (H3K4me0) to target de novo methylation, and can both promote methylation of housekeeping genes and inhibit it at bivalent gene promoters, partly by displacing DNMT3A/DNMT3B from the PRC2/EED-EZH2 complex. It plays key roles in embryonic stem cells and germ cells, where it is required with DNMT3A for methylation of imprinted loci and for silencing of retrotransposons in male germ cells. No disease association is reported in the provided data.

simple: This protein is part of the cellular machinery that copies DNA, and its job is to protect that copying process when it stalls or runs into damage, helping keep the genetic information stable. It also helps trigger the cell's safety checkpoints that pause division until problems are fixed. When it is defective, it causes severe inherited disorders marked by poor growth before birth, a very small head (microcephaly), and limb abnormalities, which can be life-threatening.
technical: DONSON encodes a replisome component that maintains genome stability by protecting stalled or damaged DNA replication forks. Following replication stress, it is required for stabilizing stalled forks, efficiently activating the intra-S-phase and G2/M cell-cycle checkpoints, and preserving genome integrity. Biallelic pathogenic variants cause autosomal recessive microcephaly with limb abnormalities, ranging from a severe lethal microcephaly-micromelia syndrome with intrauterine growth restriction, craniofacial anomalies, skeletal dysplasia, and limb malformations, to a milder phenotype with microcephaly, short stature, radial-ray limb defects, and variable developmental delay.

simple: This protein helps manage how cells move and sort their cargo within internal membrane compartments, essentially helping route materials to the right destinations inside the cell. Working with partner proteins, it helps steer certain cargo away from being broken down and instead recycled for reuse. This kind of internal traffic control supports normal cell function.
technical: DOP1B encodes protein DOP1B, which is implicated in regulating membrane trafficking of cargo proteins. Together with ATP9A and MON2, it regulates SNX3 retromer-mediated endosomal sorting of WLS, diverting it away from lysosomal degradation. Its detailed mechanism and any disease association are not established in the provided data.

simple: This protein helps nerve cells recognize and avoid their own branches, allowing them to spread out their connections neatly rather than tangling together, which is essential for building orderly neural circuits, especially in the retina of the eye. It also acts as a guidance sensor that helps growing nerve fibers find their correct paths during development of the brain and spinal cord. Through these roles it helps wire up the nervous system properly.
technical: DSCAM encodes a cell adhesion molecule that mediates neuronal self-avoidance, promoting repulsion between processes of the same cell or cell subtype to generate orderly dendritic arborization and proper mosaic spacing among retinal amacrine and ganglion cells. It also serves as a netrin (NTN1) receptor in axon guidance, acting independently of and in collaboration with DCC and possibly UNC5C, and it guides commissural axon pathfinding across the ventral midline in the developing spinal cord. It transduces signaling through activation of MAPK8 and p38 MAP kinase and promotes lamina-specific synaptic connectivity in the retina via homophilic interactions. No specific disease association is reported in the provided data.

simple: This protein is an enzyme that attaches chemical phosphate tags to other proteins, switching their activity on or off, and it helps control processes such as DNA repair, gene activity, cell survival, and brain development. Because it sits on chromosome 21, having an extra copy is thought to contribute to features of Down syndrome. When the gene is disrupted or missing, it causes a developmental disorder with a small head, severe intellectual disability, autistic behavior, and distinctive facial features.
technical: DYRK1A encodes a dual-specificity tyrosine-phosphorylation-regulated kinase with serine/threonine and tyrosine kinase activities, preferring substrates with proline at P+1 and arginine at P-3. It contributes to double-strand-break repair by phosphorylating RNF169 to promote homologous recombination, acts as a CTD kinase phosphorylating RNA polymerase II subunit POLR2A to regulate transcription, modulates alternative splicing via SRSF6, and supports cell survival including through SIRT1 phosphorylation that dampens p53/TP53-mediated apoptosis. Haploinsufficiency causes an autosomal dominant intellectual disability syndrome with primary microcephaly, severe intellectual disability without speech, anxious autistic behavior, and characteristic dysmorphic features such as bitemporal narrowing, deep-set eyes, and a pointed nasal tip.

simple: This protein is a master switch that turns genes on or off, helping control how cells behave, and it is important in blood vessel and lymphatic system development. When its gene becomes abnormally fused with another gene, it can drive cancer, notably in Ewing sarcoma, an aggressive bone and soft-tissue tumor of children and young people. Faults in this gene are also linked to a form of inherited lymphedema, a condition causing tissue swelling due to a faulty lymphatic system.
technical: ERG encodes an ETS-family transcriptional regulator that influences gene expression in part by recruiting the histone methyltransferase SETDB1 to remodel local chromatin. Chromosomal rearrangements involving ERG are oncogenic: ERG gene fusions are characteristic of Ewing sarcoma, a highly malignant primitive small round cell tumor of bone and soft tissue in children and adolescents that belongs to the neural-crest-derived Ewing sarcoma family of tumors. Germline variants are also associated with an autosomal dominant form of primary lymphedema (LMPHM14), characterized by swelling from developmental and functional defects of the lymphatic system with susceptibility to recurrent local infections.

simple: This protein, called suppressyn, comes from an ancient virus-derived sequence in our genome and plays a role in the placenta during pregnancy. It helps regulate the fusion of cells in the placenta, a process needed for the organ to develop and function properly, and it appears to act as a brake by interfering with the cell-fusion machinery. In this way it helps fine-tune placental development.
technical: ERVH48-1 encodes suppressyn, an endogenous retrovirus-derived protein implicated in trophoblast syncytialization, the fusion of trophoblast plasma membranes essential for placental development. It may negatively regulate cell-cell fusion by interacting with SLC1A5, the probable cell-surface receptor for the fusogenic syncytin-1/ERVW-1, thereby modulating syncytiotrophoblast formation. No disease association is reported in the provided data.

simple: ETS2 is a control switch inside cells that turns certain genes on. By recognizing a specific short stretch of DNA in the control regions of genes, it boosts how strongly those genes are read and used. Acting as a master switch, it helps decide which genes a cell activates, influencing how cells grow and behave.
technical: ETS2 is a sequence-specific transcription factor of the ETS family that activates transcription by binding the GGAA/T core motif (Ets-binding site, EBS) in target gene promoters. It localizes to the nucleus, where it stimulates expression of its target genes. As an ETS-family activator it participates in transcriptional programs governing cell proliferation and differentiation.

simple: EVA1C is a protein that sticks to heparin, a sugar-like molecule found on cell surfaces and in the spaces between cells. Through this binding it likely helps cells interact with their surroundings. Its detailed role in the body is not yet well understood.
technical: EVA1C (protein eva-1 homolog C) is characterized as a heparin-binding protein. The heparin-binding property suggests interactions with heparan sulfate proteoglycans of the cell surface or extracellular matrix. Beyond this binding activity its precise molecular function and pathway involvement remain incompletely defined, and no well-established disease associations are documented in the provided data.

simple: FAM207A is thought to help build ribosomes, the tiny cellular machines that manufacture proteins. Because ribosome production is essential for all cells to grow and function, this protein may play a supporting role in that process. Its exact contribution is not yet firmly established.
technical: FAM207A is the human homolog of the yeast ribosome biogenesis protein SLX9 and is proposed to function in ribosome biogenesis. Consistent with that role, it is expected to act in nucleolar/ribosome-assembly processes, though its precise mechanistic contribution remains to be clarified. No well-established disease associations are documented in the provided data.

simple: FAM243B is a predicted gene whose protein product has not been well studied. Very little is firmly known about what it does in the body. At present any description of its function would be speculative.
technical: FAM243B is an uncharacterized or predicted locus with no reviewed accession, no described molecular function, and no documented disease associations in the provided data. Nothing firm can be stated about its subcellular localization, pathway involvement, or biological role. It should be regarded as a poorly characterized gene whose protein product, if expressed, remains experimentally undefined.

simple: FAM3B is a protein that can trigger cells in the pancreas to die, including the cells that make and release hormones such as insulin. This effect grows stronger with higher amounts and longer exposure. Because of this it may be relevant to how the pancreas stays healthy.
technical: FAM3B (protein FAM3B, also known as PANDER) induces apoptosis of pancreatic alpha and beta cells in a dose- and time-dependent manner. It is a secreted protein of the FAM3 family implicated in islet-cell death and pancreatic endocrine homeostasis. No well-established disease associations are documented in the provided data.

simple: FTCD is an enzyme that helps the body process the amino acid histidine and recycle folate, a vitamin essential for many cellular reactions. It also helps organize internal scaffolding fibers near a cell's packaging center. When FTCD is faulty, histidine breakdown products build up, which can cause a blood disorder called megaloblastic anemia and, in severe cases, intellectual disability.
technical: FTCD (formimidoyltransferase-cyclodeaminase) is a folate-dependent bifunctional enzyme with both transferase and deaminase activities that channels one-carbon units from formiminoglutamate into the folate pool during histidine catabolism. It also binds and promotes bundling of vimentin filaments originating from the Golgi. Deficiency causes an autosomal recessive disorder of histidine/folate metabolism (glutamate formiminotransferase deficiency); severe forms present with histidine-induced elevation of urinary formiminoglutamate (FIGLU), megaloblastic anemia, and intellectual disability, whereas mild forms show high FIGLU excretion without histidine loading, mild developmental delay, and no hematological abnormalities.

simple: FTCD-AS1 is an antisense gene that overlaps the FTCD gene, and it does not appear to make a normal protein. Genes like this often produce regulatory RNA rather than a protein product. Little is firmly established about its role.
technical: FTCD-AS1 is annotated as an FTCD antisense locus with no reviewed protein accession, no described protein function, and no documented disease associations in the provided data. Such antisense loci typically yield long non-coding RNAs rather than a translated protein product. Nothing firm can be stated about a protein function for this gene.

simple: GABPA is a control switch that turns genes on by recognizing specific DNA sequences rich in the building blocks G and A. It helps regulate which genes are active in a cell. Among its targets, it controls a gene encoding another regulatory protein, and it can also be hijacked to activate certain viral genes.
technical: GABPA (GA-binding protein alpha chain) is an ETS-family transcription factor that binds purine-rich (GA-repeat) DNA elements to activate transcription. It positively regulates transcription of the transcriptional repressor RHIT/ZNF205 and is required for expression of the adenovirus E4 gene during viral infection. It functions in the nucleus as the DNA-binding subunit of the GABP transcriptional complex. No well-established disease associations are documented in the provided data.

simple: GART is a single protein that performs three different chemical steps in the cell's assembly line for building purines, which are essential components of DNA and RNA. Because every dividing cell needs to make new genetic material, this enzyme is important for normal cell growth. It is part of the pathway that constructs these building blocks from scratch.
technical: GART (trifunctional purine biosynthetic protein adenosine-3) is a trifunctional enzyme catalyzing three distinct steps of the de novo inosine monophosphate (IMP) biosynthetic pathway, providing purine nucleotides for DNA and RNA synthesis. It carries glycinamide ribonucleotide synthetase, phosphoribosylformylglycinamidine cyclo-ligase, and phosphoribosylglycinamide formyltransferase activities within a single polypeptide. It acts in the cytosolic de novo purine biosynthesis pathway; no well-established disease associations are documented in the provided data.

simple: GATD3 is a cleanup enzyme inside mitochondria, the cell's power plants, where it removes harmful sugar attachments that form spontaneously on proteins and nucleic acids. By doing so it prevents the buildup of damaging deposits and helps keep mitochondria working efficiently. It also helps tune a key energy-producing enzyme, supporting the cell's energy supply.
technical: GATD3 (glutamine amidotransferase-like class 1 domain-containing protein 3, mitochondrial) acts as a mitochondrial deglycase that removes early non-enzymatic glycation adducts from amino acids and nucleotides, limiting formation of advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and maintaining low glycation on mitochondrial rRNA and proteins to support mitochondrial gene expression. It contributes to mitochondrial homeostasis, respiratory capacity, and network organization. It additionally regulates malate dehydrogenase MDH2 by competitively limiting MDH2 association with the deacetylase SIRT3, thereby preserving MDH2 acetylation and activity to sustain TCA-cycle flux and oxidative phosphorylation.

simple: GATD3B is a predicted gene that appears to be a duplicate copy related to GATD3, but its own protein product is not well characterized. Because of this presumed relationship, it may resemble the mitochondrial cleanup enzyme GATD3, though this is not confirmed. Little is firmly established about it.
technical: GATD3B is an uncharacterized locus with no reviewed accession and no described function in the provided data; by its symbol it appears to be a presumed paralog/duplicate of GATD3. It is therefore predicted to resemble the GATD3 glutamine amidotransferase-like family, but specific molecular function, localization, and activity for its own product remain experimentally unestablished. No disease associations are documented, and any functional claims should be regarded as predicted rather than confirmed.

simple: GET1 helps insert a special class of proteins into the membrane of the endoplasmic reticulum, a major internal compartment where many proteins are built and processed. It works as part of a delivery system that grabs these proteins from the cell interior after they are made and anchors them correctly. This ensures the proteins end up in the right place and orientation.
technical: GET1 (guided entry of tail-anchored proteins factor 1) is required for post-translational delivery of tail-anchored (TA) proteins to the endoplasmic reticulum membrane. Together with CAMLG/GET2 it forms the ER membrane receptor for cytosolic GET3/TRC40, which captures the transmembrane domain of TA substrates, and it ensures correct topology and ER insertion of CAMLG. It is an integral ER membrane component of the GET/TRC insertion pathway; no well-established disease associations are documented in the provided data.

simple: GET1-SH3BGR is a predicted readthrough gene that spans the neighboring GET1 and SH3BGR genes, and its protein product is not well characterized. Such combined transcripts are often produced when two adjacent genes are read together. Little is firmly established about what, if anything, its protein does.
technical: GET1-SH3BGR is annotated as a readthrough locus combining the adjacent GET1 and SH3BGR genes, with no reviewed accession or described function in the provided data. Readthrough transcripts of this type may not yield a stable, functional protein, and any product would be expected only by presumed relationship to its constituent genes. No molecular function, localization, or disease association can be firmly stated for this gene.

simple: GRIK1 is a docking station on the surface of nerve cells that receives the brain's main excitatory chemical signal, glutamate. When glutamate binds, the receptor opens a channel that lets charged particles flow in, converting the chemical message into an electrical impulse that helps neurons communicate. After firing it quickly resets, helping shape signaling in the brain.
technical: GRIK1 (glutamate receptor ionotropic, kainate 1) is a cation-permeable ligand-gated ion channel of the kainate receptor subfamily, gated by L-glutamate and the agonist kainic acid. Agonist binding triggers a conformational change that opens the cation channel, transducing the chemical signal into an electrical impulse at central nervous system synapses, after which the receptor rapidly desensitizes into a transient inactive agonist-bound state. It localizes to neuronal plasma membranes at glutamatergic synapses; no well-established disease associations are documented in the provided data.

simple: H2BC12L is a histone, one of the spool-like proteins that DNA wraps around to pack the genome neatly inside the cell nucleus. This packaging controls how accessible genes are and thereby influences when genes are switched on or off, as well as DNA repair and copying. This particular histone also shows antibacterial activity and may help protect surfaces such as the gut lining.
technical: H2BC12L (histone H2B type F-S) is a core nucleosome component; nucleosomes wrap and compact DNA into chromatin, modulating DNA accessibility for transcription, repair, replication, and chromosomal stability via histone post-translational modifications and nucleosome remodeling. Beyond its structural chromatin role it has broad antibacterial activity and may contribute to the antimicrobial barrier of the colonic epithelium and to the bactericidal activity of amniotic fluid. It localizes to the nucleus as part of chromatin; no well-established disease associations are documented in the provided data.

simple: HLCS is an enzyme that attaches the vitamin biotin onto several other enzymes, switching them on so they can carry out essential steps in breaking down fats, proteins, and sugars. Without this attachment, those enzymes cannot work properly. When HLCS is faulty, babies can develop a serious metabolic disease, but symptoms often improve dramatically when given extra biotin.
technical: HLCS (holocarboxylase synthetase; biotin--protein ligase) catalyzes the biotinylation of the four biotin-dependent carboxylases: acetyl-CoA carboxylase, pyruvate carboxylase, propionyl-CoA carboxylase, and methylcrotonyl-CoA carboxylase, activating them for carboxylation reactions in central metabolism. Loss-of-function causes holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency, an autosomal recessive neonatal form of multiple carboxylase deficiency characterized by ketoacidosis, hyperammonemia, abnormal organic acid excretion, and dermatitis. Clinical and biochemical symptoms typically improve dramatically with biotin supplementation.

simple: HMGN1 is a small protein that attaches to DNA where it is wrapped around histone spools, loosening that packaging so genes can be read more easily. It is thought to help keep active genes in a state that is ready for use. It also influences chemical tags placed on histones that fine-tune gene activity.
technical: HMGN1 (non-histone chromosomal protein HMG-14) binds the inner side of nucleosomal DNA, altering DNA–histone octamer interactions and likely maintaining transcribable genes in a distinct, more accessible chromatin conformation. It inhibits phosphorylation of nucleosomal histones H3 and H2A by the kinases RPS6KA5/MSK1 and RPS6KA3/RSK2, thereby modulating the histone modification landscape. It is a nuclear chromatin-associated architectural protein; no well-established disease associations are documented in the provided data.

simple: HSF2BP helps cells correctly repair breaks in DNA during the special cell divisions that produce eggs and sperm, ensuring chromosomes pair up and exchange material properly. This makes it essential for fertility. In women, faulty versions of this protein are linked to the ovaries stopping work prematurely, leading to early loss of menstrual cycles.
technical: HSF2BP (heat shock factor 2-binding protein) is a meiotic recombination factor that, through interaction with BRCA2, modulates loading of the DMC1:RAD51 recombinases at meiotic double-strand break (DSB) sites, and is required for DSB repair, homologous synapsis, and crossover formation needed to progress past metaphase I. It is essential for spermatogenesis and male fertility and for proper recombinase recruitment in female meiosis; it can also sequester BNC1 in the cytoplasm to inhibit its transcriptional activity and may modulate HSF2 activation in testis. In humans, biallelic loss-of-function is associated with premature ovarian failure (POF19), an autosomal recessive form with irregular cycles and cessation of menstruation in the third decade, with elevated serum gonadotropins and low estradiol.

simple: HSPA13 belongs to the family of heat shock proteins, cellular helpers that use chemical energy to assist other proteins. This particular member breaks down the cell's energy molecule ATP and is thought to support normal protein handling inside cells. Its precise everyday role is still only partly understood.
technical: HSPA13 is a member of the HSP70 family that displays peptide-independent ATPase activity. Unlike classical HSP70 chaperones whose ATPase cycle is stimulated by substrate peptides, its activity does not depend on bound peptide. No well-established human disease associations are documented for this protein.

simple: HUNK is an enzyme called a kinase, meaning it adds chemical tags to other proteins to switch their activity on or off. Kinases like this one help relay signals that control how cells grow and behave. The specific tasks of HUNK in the body are not yet firmly established.
technical: HUNK (hormonally up-regulated neu-associated kinase) is a serine/threonine protein kinase named for its expression pattern, but no detailed molecular function is annotated in the provided record. As a kinase it is presumed to act in intracellular signal transduction through substrate phosphorylation. No well-established human disease associations are documented here, and specific pathway and substrate claims should be treated as provisional.

simple: ICOSLG sits on the surface of immune and other cells and acts as a partner signal that helps activate T cells, key players in the body's defenses. By switching on this partner signal, it boosts T-cell multiplication, encourages B cells to mature into antibody-making cells, and helps coordinate inflammation. When this gene is faulty, children can suffer recurrent infections and a weakened immune system because they make too few antibodies and immune cells.
technical: ICOSLG (ICOS ligand, also B7-H2) is a cell-surface costimulatory molecule that engages the T-cell receptor ICOS to drive T-cell proliferation and cytokine secretion, and it also promotes B-cell proliferation and differentiation into plasma cells. It contributes to local inflammatory responses and to memory T-cell costimulation, and in endothelial cells it is required for neutrophil transmigration in response to chemoattractants such as CXCL8/IL8 or fMLP. Biallelic loss-of-function variants cause an autosomal recessive immunodeficiency featuring childhood-onset recurrent respiratory infections, susceptibility to chronic DNA viral infections, hypogammaglobulinemia, and panlymphopenia.

simple: IFNAR1 is part of a cell-surface antenna that detects type I interferons, alarm signals the body releases to fight viruses. When interferon docks, this receptor triggers an internal relay that switches on antiviral defense genes. People who lack a working version are unusually vulnerable to viral infections and can have dangerous reactions to live virus vaccines such as measles, mumps, rubella, and yellow fever.
technical: IFNAR1 is one of the two subunits (with IFNAR2) of the type I interferon receptor, which binds interferons alpha, beta, epsilon, omega, and kappa. Ligand binding brings IFNAR1 and IFNAR2 into proximity, allowing their associated Janus kinases (TYK2 on IFNAR1, JAK1 on IFNAR2) to cross-phosphorylate and create docking sites for STAT transcription factors, activating the JAK-STAT cascade and interferon-stimulated gene expression; IFNAR1 can also form an active IFNB1 receptor independently of IFNAR2. Autosomal recessive deficiency causes an immunodeficiency (IMD106) with increased susceptibility to viral infection beginning in infancy, severe adverse reactions to live attenuated viral vaccines, and predisposition to severe SARS-CoV-2 respiratory disease.

simple: IFNAR2 is the second half of the cell-surface antenna that senses type I interferons, the body's antiviral alarm signals. Together with its partner it relays the alarm into the cell to turn on virus-fighting genes. People who lack a working copy are highly susceptible to viral infections and can develop life-threatening brain inflammation after routine measles, mumps, and rubella vaccination.
technical: IFNAR2 partners with IFNAR1 to form the heterodimeric type I interferon receptor for interferons alpha, beta, epsilon, omega, and kappa. Ligand binding juxtaposes the two subunits so their associated kinases (TYK2 on IFNAR1, JAK1 on IFNAR2) cross-phosphorylate, generating tyrosine docking sites for STAT1, STAT2, and STAT3, which then translocate to the nucleus to regulate interferon-stimulated genes; a soluble form can also act as a potent inhibitor of type I IFN receptor activity. Autosomal recessive deficiency causes impaired antiviral immunity with infection-associated encephalopathy and risk of fatal encephalitis following MMR vaccination.

simple: This entry refers to a read-through arrangement spanning the neighboring IFNAR2 and IL10RB genes rather than a single well-defined protein. Because no protein record or functional information is available, little is firmly established about any product it might encode. It should be regarded as a predicted or poorly characterized locus.
technical: IFNAR2-IL10RB denotes a predicted read-through transcript involving the adjacent IFNAR2 and IL10RB cytokine-receptor genes; no accession, name, function, or disease annotation is provided. The two parent genes encode type I interferon and class 2 cytokine receptor subunits, but the existence and function of a distinct read-through protein product are not firmly established. This should be treated as an unresolved or poorly characterized locus, and no specific molecular, localization, or disease claims can be made.

simple: IFNGR2 is part of the cell-surface receptor for interferon gamma, an immune signal that is especially important for fighting bacteria like tuberculosis. This piece is needed to pass the signal into the cell once interferon gamma is captured. When it does not work, people become dangerously susceptible to mycobacterial infections, including from the BCG vaccine and tuberculosis bacteria.
technical: IFNGR2 associates with the ligand-binding subunit IFNGR1 to form the interferon-gamma receptor, where IFNGR2 is required for signal transduction rather than for ligand binding. Receptor engagement activates the JAK/STAT signaling pathway to drive interferon-gamma-responsive gene expression critical for antimycobacterial immunity. Autosomal recessive deficiency causes Mendelian susceptibility to mycobacterial disease (IMD28), with early-onset, often fatal infections from weakly virulent mycobacteria such as BCG and environmental species, as well as Mycobacterium tuberculosis and occasionally Salmonella.

simple: IGSF5 is an adhesion protein that helps cells stick together and seal the gaps between them, contributing to barriers in tissues such as the kidney filter and the intestinal lining. It can bind matching copies of itself on neighboring cells to hold them together without needing calcium. In the testis it appears to help different cell types stay connected during sperm development.
technical: IGSF5 (immunoglobulin superfamily member 5, JAM4) mediates calcium-independent homophilic cell adhesion and, together with MAGI1, forms an adhesion complex at tight junctions that may regulate permeability of the kidney glomerulus and small intestinal epithelium. In testis it appears to act as a cell adhesion molecule rather than a tight-junction component, potentially mediating adhesion among spermatogonia and Sertoli cells. No well-established human disease associations are documented for this protein.

simple: IL10RB is a shared cell-surface receptor part used by several immune signaling molecules, including the anti-inflammatory signal interleukin-10 and interferons that defend against viruses. It helps cells receive these messages and mount appropriate immune and antiviral responses. Defects affecting this pathway are linked to inflammatory bowel disease, a chronic inflammation of the digestive tract.
technical: IL10RB is a shared receptor subunit required for signaling by five class 2 cytokines (IL10, IL22, IL26, IL28, and IFNL1); paired with IFNLR1 it forms the receptor for IFNL2 and IFNL3 to mediate antiviral activity. Ligand binding activates the JAK/STAT pathway, inducing interferon-stimulated genes and contributing to the antiviral state. It is associated with inflammatory bowel disease, a chronic relapsing gastrointestinal inflammation comprising Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis phenotypes with potential extraintestinal manifestations.

simple: ITGB2 is the shared building block of several adhesion molecules on white blood cells that let them grab onto vessel walls and crawl into tissues to fight infection. By pairing with different partners it recognizes various targets and helps immune cells stick, move, and engulf debris and germs. When it is missing or faulty, white blood cells cannot reach sites of infection, leading to recurrent, serious bacterial infections.
technical: ITGB2 (integrin beta-2, CD18) pairs with alpha subunits (ITGAL, ITGAM, ITGAX, ITGAD) to form leukocyte integrins that bind ligands including ICAM1-5, iC3b complement fragment, fibrinogen, factor X, VCAM1, and ISG15, mediating leukocyte adhesion, tethering, rolling, and transmigration. These complexes function at the immunological synapse, support natural killer cell cytotoxicity, neutrophil activation and transmigration, erythrophagocytosis, and macrophage clearance of apoptotic neutrophils. Deficiency causes leukocyte adhesion deficiency type 1 (LAD1), characterized by recurrent bacterial infections and impaired adhesion-dependent leukocyte functions.

simple: ITSN1 is a scaffolding protein that links the machinery cells use to take in material from their surface with the system that builds the cell's internal skeleton. It helps form the vesicles that internalize receptors and recycles signaling proteins, and in nerve cells it supports the rapid recycling of the bubbles that release neurotransmitters. Through these roles it helps keep cell signaling and communication running smoothly.
technical: ITSN1 (intersectin-1) is an adapter linking endocytic membrane traffic to the actin assembly machinery, acting as a guanine nucleotide exchange factor for CDC42 to stimulate WASL/ARP2/3-mediated actin nucleation. It functions in assembly and maturation of clathrin-coated vesicles (recruiting FCHSD2 to clathrin-coated pits) and in endocytosis of activated EGFR, integrin beta-1, and transferrin receptor, also promoting EGFR ubiquitination and degradation. At presynaptic active zones it is required for synaptic vesicle endocytosis and replenishment of release-ready vesicles, and it inhibits ARHGAP31 activity toward RAC1. No well-established human disease associations are documented in the provided record.

simple: JAM2 is an adhesion protein at the junctions between cells that helps cells recognize and hold onto one another. It guides white blood cells as they stick to and pass through blood vessel walls, helps anchor blood-forming stem cells in the bone marrow, and supports sperm development. Faults in this gene are linked to a brain disorder marked by abnormal calcium deposits in deep brain regions, which can cause a range of movement and neuropsychiatric symptoms.
technical: JAM2 (junctional adhesion molecule B) mediates heterotypic adhesion with its receptor JAM3 to regulate diverse processes, including retention of JAM3-expressing hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells on bone marrow stromal cells. It facilitates leukocyte tethering, rolling (via integrin alpha-4/beta-1 binding), and transmigration across endothelium, contributes to Sertoli-germ cell anchorage during spermatogenesis, acts as an inhibitory cue limiting non-axonal myelination, and participates in myocyte fusion and angiogenesis. Biallelic variants cause a form of primary familial brain calcification with symmetric basal ganglia and other calcifications and variable neuropsychiatric features, with normal calcium-phosphate metabolism.

simple: KCNE1 is a small helper subunit that partners with potassium channels, the pores that let potassium ions flow across cell membranes to control electrical activity in the heart and inner ear. By tuning how these channels open and close, it helps the heart's electrical cycle reset properly. Faulty versions can cause inherited heart-rhythm disorders, sometimes combined with congenital deafness, that raise the risk of fainting and sudden death.
technical: KCNE1 is an ancillary beta subunit that regulates voltage-gated potassium (Kv) channels, modulating gating kinetics and stabilizing the channel complex. It assembles with KCNQ1/KvLQT1 to form the slowly activating delayed rectifier cardiac potassium current (IKs) underlying ventricular repolarization, and it also modulates KCNB1- and KCNH2/HERG-containing channels.
