Mutations cause long QT syndrome with prolonged QT interval and polymorphic ventricular arrhythmias, including an autosomal recessive form (Jervell and Lange-Nielsen) combining congenital deafness with QT prolongation, syncope, and high risk of sudden death.

simple: Based on its name, KCNE1B appears to be a close relative of the KCNE1 potassium-channel helper gene, but no protein record or functional data are provided here. It is therefore predicted to resemble that family rather than firmly characterized. Little can be stated with confidence about its actual protein product.
technical: KCNE1B is presumed from its symbol to be a KCNE1-related paralog within the KCNE family of voltage-gated potassium channel ancillary (beta) subunits, but no accession, name, function, or disease annotation is provided. By analogy to KCNE1 it might be predicted to act as a regulatory subunit modulating Kv channel gating, though this is unverified. It should be treated as a predicted or poorly characterized gene, and no specific molecular, localization, or disease claims can be substantiated.

simple: KCNE2 is a small helper subunit that partners with potassium channels to fine-tune the flow of potassium ions that controls electrical activity in the heart and other tissues. By adjusting how these channels open, close, and stay stable, it helps keep the heartbeat regular. Faulty versions are linked to inherited heart-rhythm disorders, including long QT syndrome and atrial fibrillation, which can cause fainting, stroke, and sudden death.
technical: KCNE2 is an ancillary beta subunit that modulates the gating kinetics and stability of voltage-gated potassium (Kv) channels. It associates with KCNH2/HERG to shape the rapidly activating delayed rectifier current (IKr), modulates KCNB1-, KCNQ1-, KCNQ2/3-, and HCN-containing channels, and forms a KCNQ1-KCNE2 complex implicated in choroid plexus myo-inositol transport affecting neuronal excitability. Mutations are associated with long QT syndrome (prolonged QT with polymorphic ventricular arrhythmias and risk of sudden death) and with familial atrial fibrillation, which predisposes to palpitations, syncope, thromboembolic stroke, and heart failure.

simple: KCNJ15 forms an inward-rectifier potassium channel, a pore that lets potassium ions move more easily into cells than out, helping set the cell's electrical baseline. Its opening responds to the level of potassium just outside the cell. No firmly established human disease is linked to this protein in the available information.
technical: KCNJ15 is an ATP-sensitive inward rectifier potassium (Kir) channel that conducts potassium preferentially inward, with voltage dependence modulated by extracellular potassium concentration such that higher external potassium shifts the activation range to more positive voltages. Inward rectification arises mainly from block of outward current by intracellular magnesium. No well-established human disease associations are documented in the provided record.

simple: KCNJ6 forms a potassium channel that is switched on by G proteins, the internal relays activated by many hormone and neurotransmitter receptors. By letting potassium flow into cells, it helps calm electrical activity and may influence processes such as insulin release. Disruption of this channel is linked to a rare disorder involving severe developmental delay, intellectual disability, and loss of body fat, along with distinctive facial features.
technical: KCNJ6 (GIRK2) is a G protein-activated inward rectifier potassium channel whose voltage dependence is set by extracellular potassium and whose inward rectification reflects block of outward current by intracellular magnesium. Activated downstream of G protein-coupled receptors, it contributes to regulation of cellular excitability and may participate in glucose- and neurotransmitter-modulated insulin secretion. It is associated with a rare syndrome featuring severe developmental delay, intellectual disability, generalized lipodystrophy, and dysmorphic features including microcephaly, prominent eyes, and an aged appearance.

simple: KRTAP10-1 is a structural protein found in hair. It binds and cross-links the keratin fibers that make up the hair shaft, using its many sulfur-containing building blocks to form strong chemical bridges that give hair its rigidity and resistance. No human disease is linked to this protein in the available information.
technical: KRTAP10-1 is a high-sulfur keratin-associated protein of the hair cortex, where hair keratin intermediate filaments are embedded in an interfilamentous matrix of KRTAPs. Through extensive disulfide cross-linking via its abundant cysteine residues, it interconnects hair keratins to form a rigid, resistant hair shaft. No well-established human disease associations are documented for this protein.

simple: This protein is part of the structural glue that holds hair together. Hair fibers are built from tough strands called keratins, and proteins like this one wrap around those strands and lock them in place using sulfur-rich chemical bonds, giving hair its strength, stiffness, and resistance to wear.
technical: KRTAP10-10 is a high-sulfur keratin-associated protein expressed in the hair cortex. No specific disease associations are firmly established for this family member.

simple: This protein helps fill the space between the fibers that make up a hair strand. It ties those fibers together using sulfur-based chemical bonds, helping make the hair strong and durable. It belongs to a large family of hair-building proteins.
technical: Keratin-associated protein 7-1 (KRTAP7-1) is a member of the KRTAP family forming the interfilamentous matrix that embeds hair keratin intermediate filaments in the hair cortex. Its cysteine-rich composition allows extensive disulfide cross-linking with hair keratins, contributing to a rigid and resistant hair shaft. It is expressed in the cortex of the hair follicle. No specific disease associations are well established for this protein.

simple: This is a small protein that acts as part of the matrix holding a hair strand together. It bonds to the keratin fibers with sulfur-rich links, helping form a strong and resilient hair shaft. It is one of a large family of similar hair proteins.
technical: Keratin-associated protein 8-1 (KRTAP8-1) belongs to the high-glycine-tyrosine/high-sulfur KRTAP family that makes up the interfilamentous matrix of the hair cortex. Through disulfide cross-linking mediated by its cysteine residues, it binds hair keratin intermediate filaments, supporting the rigidity and resistance of the hair shaft. Expression is restricted to the hair follicle cortex. No specific disease associations are well established for this protein.

simple: This protein is a close relative of lebercilin, a protein found in the tiny hair-like structures (cilia) on cells, including those in the eye. Based on that family relationship, it is thought to play a role in these cilia, though its exact job has not been firmly pinned down. Its specific functions and any links to disease remain to be clearly established.
technical: LCA5L (lebercilin-like protein) is a paralog of lebercilin (LCA5), a ciliary protein associated with the connecting cilium of photoreceptors and the broader ciliary/centrosomal apparatus. By homology it is presumed to be a cilia-associated protein, but no specific molecular function, defined subcellular localization, or pathway has been firmly experimentally established for LCA5L. Unlike LCA5, which is linked to Leber congenital amaurosis, no well-established disease association is documented for LCA5L; claims about its precise role should remain conservative.

simple: This protein is an enzyme that acts on certain fats in the body. It specifically breaks down a lipid called phosphatidic acid to produce lysophosphatidic acid, a small fat molecule that acts as a signaling messenger between cells. By generating this messenger, the enzyme can influence processes such as cell growth and movement.
technical: LIPI (lipase member I) is a member of the lipase family that specifically hydrolyzes phosphatidic acid (PA) to generate 2-acyl lysophosphatidic acid (LPA), a potent bioactive lipid signaling mediator, plus free fatty acid. It is highly substrate-specific, not acting on phosphatidylserine, phosphatidylcholine, phosphatidylethanolamine, or triacylglycerol. Through LPA production it can feed into LPA receptor signaling pathways that regulate cellular proliferation and motility. No well-established disease association is firmly documented for this enzyme.

simple: This protein belongs to a large family of proteins built from repeated, curved building blocks that are typically good at sticking to other proteins. Its specific job in the body has not been clearly worked out, so its exact role and importance remain uncertain.
technical: LRRC3 (Leucine-rich repeat-containing protein 3) is named for its leucine-rich repeat (LRR) motifs, structural modules that commonly mediate protein-protein interactions. No specific molecular function, subcellular localization, or pathway has been firmly established in the provided data, and there are no documented disease associations. Functional characterization remains limited, so claims beyond the general properties of LRR-containing proteins should be treated as conservative inferences.

simple: This enzyme performs a key step in making cholesterol, a fat-like substance the body needs for cell membranes and to build hormones. It folds a precursor molecule into the basic ring-shaped core shared by all sterols, and in the eye its product may help keep the lens clear. When the gene is faulty, it can cause clouding of the lens (cataracts) and very sparse or absent hair, sometimes alongside skin and developmental problems.
technical: LSS (lanosterol synthase) is a key enzyme of the cholesterol biosynthesis pathway that catalyzes the cyclization of (S)-2,3-oxidosqualene to lanosterol, forming the sterol nucleus. Through lanosterol production it may regulate lens protein aggregation and promote lens transparency. Loss-of-function variants are associated with autosomal recessive cataract (lens opacification leading to visual impairment or blindness) and with hypotrichosis (HYPT14), characterized by sparse to absent scalp hair, brittle eyebrows, and sparse eyelashes and body hair; an associated recessive disorder features alopecia universalis, scaly skin, variable intellectual disability, and motor and speech delay.

simple: When a cell's protein-making machinery gets stuck partway through building a protein, this protein tags the unfinished, faulty product for disposal. By marking these defective proteins for breakdown, it helps keep the cell free of harmful protein junk. This quality-control role is important for keeping cells, including nerve cells, healthy.
technical: LTN1 (E3 ubiquitin-protein ligase listerin) is the E3 ligase component of the ribosome quality control complex (RQC), which ubiquitinates and targets incompletely synthesized nascent chains for proteasomal degradation. Recruited to stalled 60S ribosomal subunits by NEMF, LTN1 ubiquitinates the arrested nascent polypeptide, after which VCP/p97 mediates extraction and degradation of the incomplete product. It functions in ribosome-associated co-translational protein quality control; no human disease association is documented in the provided data.

simple: This protein is named for its resemblance to part of a known cell-signaling enzyme, but its own specific job has not been clearly established. Because little is documented about it, its precise role and importance to health remain uncertain.
technical: MAP3K7CL (MAP3K7 C-terminal-like protein) is named for similarity to the C-terminal region of the kinase MAP3K7 (TAK1), but the provided data report no defined molecular function, subcellular localization, or pathway. No disease associations are documented. Functional characterization is limited, so any inferred role based on the name should be regarded as tentative.

simple: This protein helps move newly made genetic messages (mRNA) out of the cell's nucleus so they can be used to build proteins, and it also helps control DNA copying so it happens just once per cell division. It additionally tweaks how DNA is packaged to help fine-tune antibody genes in the immune system. When the gene is defective, it can cause an inherited nerve disorder with muscle weakness and wasting in the hands and feet, sometimes with learning difficulties.
technical: MCM3AP (germinal-center associated nuclear protein, GANP) is a component of the TREX-2 complex that mediates nuclear export of mRNAs through the nuclear pore. It binds and acetylates the replication protein MCM3 and contributes to controls that restrict DNA replication initiation to once per cell cycle, and through histone acetylation it modulates nucleosome assembly at immunoglobulin variable region genes to favor AICDA/AID targeting and somatic hypermutation. Biallelic loss-of-function variants cause an autosomal recessive peripheral sensorimotor neuropathy with early-childhood onset, progressive distal muscle weakness and atrophy of hands and feet, gait difficulties, and frequently impaired intellectual development.

simple: This protein helps mark the special region of each chromosome that the cell uses as a handle to pull chromosomes apart during cell division. By recruiting a key marker protein to that region, it helps ensure each new cell gets the correct set of chromosomes. This makes it important for accurate cell division.
technical: MIS18A (protein Mis18-alpha) is required for the recruitment of the centromeric histone variant CENPA (CENP-A) to centromeres, thereby licensing centromere identity for proper kinetochore assembly. It functions in centromere maintenance and is necessary for normal chromosome segregation during mitosis. No disease association is documented in the provided data.

simple: This protein helps the body defend against viruses by switching on antiviral immune signals and limiting the spread of several different viruses. It works inside the cell nucleus, where it also reads marks on the DNA-packaging proteins and helps regulate a major guardian protein that controls cell health. Through these roles it contributes to innate immunity and the cell's response to infection.
technical: MORC3 (MORC family CW-type zinc finger protein 3) is a nuclear matrix protein that forms ATP-dependent nuclear bodies (MORC3-NBs) and contributes to innate immunity by restricting diverse viruses via modulation of the interferon (IFN) response, including activation of IFNB1. It is a histone methylation reader that binds histone H3 'Lys-4' with a preference order H3K4me3 > H3K4me2 > H3K4me1 > H3K4me0, and its sumoylated nuclear bodies associate with PML-NBs and recruit TP53 and SP100 to regulate TP53 activity. It binds RNA in vitro and may be required for influenza A transcription during infection; no human disease association is documented in the provided data.

simple: This small helper protein assists a family of cell-surface receptors that respond to hormones, most importantly the receptor for ACTH, the hormone that tells the adrenal glands to make the stress hormone cortisol. It helps that receptor reach the cell surface and respond properly. When it is missing, the adrenal glands cannot respond to ACTH, leading to a serious cortisol deficiency that often appears in newborns or young children.
technical: MRAP (melanocortin-2 receptor accessory protein) is a modulator of melanocortin receptors (MC1R-MC5R) that increases their ligand sensitivity and enhances cAMP generation. It is required for trafficking of MC2R to the cell surface of adrenal cells and for signaling in response to ACTH (corticotropin), and may participate in intracellular trafficking in adipocytes. Loss-of-function variants cause an autosomal recessive form of familial glucocorticoid deficiency, characterized by adrenal resistance to ACTH, low cortisol with elevated ACTH and normal aldosterone/renin, and neonatal or early-childhood hypoglycemia, failure to thrive, recurrent infections, and risk of shock.

simple: This protein is a building block of the protein-making machinery found inside mitochondria, the cell's energy-producing compartments. It helps mitochondria manufacture the proteins they need to generate energy. When the gene is faulty, it can cause an inherited mitochondrial disease that ranges from a severe, life-threatening condition in infancy to milder problems such as heart muscle enlargement and metabolic disturbances.
technical: MRPL39 (large ribosomal subunit protein mL39) is a component of the large subunit of the mitochondrial ribosome (mitoribosome), which carries out translation of mitochondrially encoded proteins essential for oxidative phosphorylation. Biallelic variants cause an autosomal recessive mitochondrial disease with multisystem manifestations of variable severity, spanning from lethal infantile Leigh syndrome to a milder phenotype with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, lactic acidosis, attention deficit-hyperactivity disorder, and survival into adulthood. Its localization is mitochondrial.

simple: This protein is a building block of the small part of the protein-making machinery inside mitochondria, the cell's energy factories. It helps mitochondria produce the proteins needed for generating energy. Its specific individual role beyond being part of this machinery is not detailed here, and no disease link is documented.
technical: MRPS6 (small ribosomal subunit protein bS6m) is a component of the small subunit of the mitochondrial ribosome, which mediates translation of mitochondrially encoded polypeptides for the oxidative phosphorylation system. Its localization is mitochondrial. No specific molecular detail beyond mitoribosomal subunit membership or disease association is provided in the source data.

simple: This protein is switched on by interferon, an alarm signal the body releases during infection, and it acts as a broad antiviral defender against many viruses. It works by trapping or disabling key viral components so the virus cannot copy itself, including influenza and hepatitis B viruses. This makes it an important part of the body's first-line defense against viral infection.
technical: MX1 (interferon-induced GTP-binding protein Mx1) is an interferon-induced dynamin-like GTPase with broad antiviral activity against many RNA viruses and some DNA viruses. It binds and inactivates viral ribonucleocapsids, blocks nuclear import of viral nucleocapsids (e.g., Thogoto virus), sequesters viral nucleoprotein in perinuclear complexes to prevent genome amplification and egress (e.g., La Crosse virus), and inhibits influenza A virus by impairing NP synthesis and endocytic trafficking of incoming particles; it may also enhance ER stress-mediated cell death after influenza infection and regulate TRPC channel activity. It functions in interferon-driven innate antiviral immunity; no human disease association is documented in the provided data.

simple: This protein is turned on by interferon, an infection-warning signal, and is a potent defender against HIV-1, the virus that causes AIDS. It blocks the virus by targeting its protein shell and preventing the virus from getting its genetic material into the cell's nucleus and inserting it into our DNA. It is therefore an important natural barrier against HIV infection.
technical: MX2 (interferon-induced GTP-binding protein Mx2) is an interferon-induced dynamin-like GTPase with potent antiviral activity against HIV-1. It targets the viral capsid and affects nuclear uptake and/or stability of the HIV-1 replication complex and subsequent chromosomal integration of proviral DNA, and also shows activity against simian immunodeficiency virus (SIV-mnd). It may additionally regulate nucleocytoplasmic transport and cell-cycle progression; no human disease association is documented in the provided data.

simple: This enzyme attaches small chemical tags (methyl groups) onto certain proteins, which helps fine-tune how genes and other proteins behave. It also plays a minor part in processing arsenic, helping convert one form into a less toxic one. Its main, best-established job is modifying specific proteins involved in gene regulation and the cell's protein-building quality control.
technical: N6AMT1 (methyltransferase HEMK2) is a methyltransferase that, as the catalytic subunit of a heterodimer with TRMT112, monomethylates histone H4 'Lys-12' (H4K12me1) at promoters of cell-cycle regulator genes and catalyzes N5-methylation of a glutamine residue in proteins bearing a Gly-Gln-Xaa-Xaa-Xaa-Arg motif, including methylation of the translation termination factor ETF1 (eRF1) at 'Gln-185' (efficient when ETF1 is complexed with GTP-bound ERF3). It can also methylate arsenic, converting monomethylarsonous acid (3+) toward less toxic dimethylarsonic acid, though it plays only a limited role in arsenic metabolism relative to AS3MT. No disease association is documented in the provided data.

simple: This protein sits on the surface of nerve cells and helps them recognize and connect with the right partners. It appears especially important for guiding the wiring of the sense of smell, helping olfactory nerve fibers bundle together and reach their correct targets. In this way it contributes to building accurate connections in the nervous system.
technical: NCAM2 (neural cell adhesion molecule 2) is a cell-surface adhesion molecule that may play important roles in selective fasciculation and zone-to-zone projection of primary olfactory axons. It functions in neural cell adhesion and axon guidance within the developing olfactory system. No disease association is documented in the provided data.

simple: This protein is a small helper part of Complex I, a large machine inside mitochondria that begins the process of turning nutrients into cellular energy. It is not thought to do the chemistry itself but supports the assembly or stability of the complex. It contributes to the energy-generating respiratory chain in the cell's powerhouses.
technical: NDUFV3 (NADH dehydrogenase [ubiquinone] flavoprotein 3, mitochondrial) is an accessory subunit of mitochondrial respiratory chain Complex I (NADH:ubiquinone oxidoreductase) that is believed not to participate directly in catalysis. Complex I transfers electrons from NADH to ubiquinone as part of oxidative phosphorylation, and NDUFV3 may be a terminally assembled subunit of the complex. Its localization is the inner mitochondrial membrane; no disease association is documented in the provided data.

simple: This protein helps control how cells respond to hormones such as estrogen and stress hormones by adjusting the activity of the receptors that sense them. It also helps regulate the body's internal clock genes and is involved in the development of the kidneys and ovaries. When the gene is altered, it can contribute to birth malformations of the kidneys and urinary tract, a leading cause of chronic kidney disease in children.
technical: NRIP1 (nuclear receptor-interacting protein 1, RIP140) modulates transcriptional activation by steroid receptors such as NR3C1, NR3C2, and ESR1, and also modulates transcriptional repression by nuclear hormone receptors. It is a positive regulator of circadian clock gene expression, acting as a coactivator for RORA and RORC to stimulate transcription of BMAL1, CLOCK, and CRY1, and is implicated in ovarian function and renal development. Variants are associated with congenital anomalies of the kidney and urinary tract (CAKUT3, autosomal dominant), encompassing renal agenesis, kidney hypodysplasia, multicystic dysplasia, duplex collecting system, posterior urethral valves, and ureteral abnormalities.

simple: OLIG1 is a master switch that helps decide what kind of cells form in the developing brain and spinal cord. Working together with its partner OLIG2, it pushes immature cells to become the cells that insulate nerve fibers, while blocking them from turning into certain other brain cell types. This kind of decision-making is important for building healthy nervous tissue and is also relevant to how some brain tumors arise.
technical: OLIG1 is a basic helix-loop-helix transcription factor that orchestrates progenitor cell fate decisions during cortical gliogenesis and gliomagenesis. In cooperation with OLIG2, it activates the formation of oligodendrocyte precursor cells while repressing the generation of olfactory bulb interneuron intermediate progenitors, in part by binding and silencing conserved enhancer elements of GSX2. It also cooperates with OLIG2 to establish the pMN domain of the embryonic neural tube. As a sequence-specific DNA-binding regulator, it acts in the nucleus to control neural lineage specification.

simple: OLIG2 is a control protein that tells immature cells in the spinal cord and brain to become nerve-insulating cells or motor neurons, the cells that command our muscles. It works alongside related proteins to steer these developmental choices. Because it governs cell identity in the nervous system, it is also studied in the context of brain tumors.
technical: OLIG2 is a basic helix-loop-helix transcription factor required for oligodendrocyte and motor neuron specification in the spinal cord and for somatic motor neuron development in the hindbrain, acting in coordination with ZNF488 to promote oligodendrocyte differentiation. It orchestrates progenitor cell fates during cortical gliogenesis and gliomagenesis, cooperating to activate oligodendrocyte precursor cell formation while repressing olfactory bulb interneuron intermediate progenitors through transcriptional regulation of GSX2. It cooperates with OLIG1 to establish the pMN domain of the embryonic neural tube and antagonizes V2 and NKX2-2-induced V3 interneuron development. As a nuclear DNA-binding regulator, it is a central determinant of ventral neural tube patterning.

simple: PAXBP1 acts as a connector that links muscle-building control proteins to the machinery that switches genes on and off. By bringing these pieces together, it helps regulate how muscle progenitor cells multiply during the formation of muscle tissue. This makes it part of the normal program for building skeletal muscle.
technical: PAXBP1 is an adapter protein that links the transcription factors PAX3 and PAX7 to the histone methylation machinery and participates in myogenesis. It associates with a histone methyltransferase complex that mediates di- and trimethylation of histone H3 Lys-4, and it mediates recruitment of that complex to PAX3 and PAX7 on chromatin. Through this mechanism it regulates expression of genes governing muscle progenitor cell proliferation, including ID3 and CDC20, functioning in the nucleus at the interface of transcription factor binding and chromatin modification.

simple: PCBP3 is a protein that attaches to single-stranded genetic material, showing a preference for stretches rich in one of the building blocks (cytosine). Proteins of this kind typically help manage how genetic messages are handled inside cells. Its precise roles in the body are not yet fully characterized.
technical: PCBP3 is a poly(rC)-binding protein that binds single-stranded nucleic acids, with a preference for oligo dC sequences. It belongs to the KH-domain family of poly(rC)-binding proteins, which generally participate in post-transcriptional regulation such as mRNA stability and translational control. Beyond this sequence-specific single-stranded nucleic acid binding activity, its detailed cellular functions and pathway associations remain incompletely defined; no well-established disease associations are documented.

simple: Pericentrin is a scaffolding protein at the centrosome, the cell's organizing hub for the internal fibers that pull chromosomes apart when a cell divides. By anchoring this machinery, it helps cells divide correctly during growth and reproduction. When it is defective, people can develop a rare inherited form of severe dwarfism, with adults reaching about one meter in height and a small brain, yet near-normal intelligence.
technical: Pericentrin (PCNT) is an integral component of the filamentous pericentriolar material of the centrosome and is involved in the initial establishment of organized microtubule arrays during mitosis and meiosis. It contributes, together with DISC1, to microtubule network formation and may help prevent premature centrosome splitting during interphase by inhibiting NEK2 kinase activity at the centrosome. Loss-of-function mutations cause a rare autosomal recessive primordial dwarfism in which affected adults reach an average height of about 100 centimeters and have a markedly small brain comparable to that of a 3-month-old infant, but near-normal intelligence.

simple: PCP4 is a small protein that fine-tunes calmodulin, a key sensor cells use to respond to calcium signals. By adjusting how calmodulin works, it helps shape downstream signaling, including pathways involved in the maturation of nerve cells. It is found largely in the nervous system.
technical: PCP4 (PEP-19) is a calmodulin regulator that modulates calcium binding by calmodulin, thereby tuning calmodulin activity and the diverse processes it controls. By influencing calmodulin, it can affect calmodulin-dependent kinase signaling pathways, and it has been implicated in neuronal differentiation. It functions as a small intrinsically disordered IQ-motif protein acting on calmodulin-mediated calcium signaling; no well-established disease associations are documented.

simple: PDE9A is an enzyme that breaks down cGMP, a small signaling molecule cells use to pass along messages. By clearing cGMP, it helps control processes in the heart, such as the response to stress that can lead to heart enlargement, and in the brain it contributes to learning and long-term memory. Because of these roles, it has drawn interest as a drug target.
technical: PDE9A is a cyclic nucleotide phosphodiesterase that specifically hydrolyzes the second messenger cGMP, and it has the highest affinity and selectivity for cGMP among the PDE family. In the heart it specifically regulates natriuretic-peptide-dependent cGMP signaling, acting as a regulator of cardiac hypertrophy in myocytes and muscle, without affecting nitric oxide-dependent cGMP pools. In the brain it has been linked to cognitive functions such as learning and long-term memory. By terminating cGMP signaling, it shapes the spatial and temporal dynamics of this pathway; no well-established Mendelian disease associations are documented.

simple: Pyridoxal kinase activates vitamin B6 by adding a phosphate group, converting the dietary forms of the vitamin into PLP, the form the body can actually use. PLP is an essential helper for well over a hundred chemical reactions, so this step is vital for normal metabolism. When this enzyme is faulty, people can develop an inherited nerve disorder featuring progressive weakness in the legs, walking difficulty, pain, and gradual loss of vision.
technical: PDXK (pyridoxal kinase) catalyzes phosphorylation of the dietary vitamin B6 vitamers pyridoxal, pyridoxine, and pyridoxamine to form pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP), pyridoxine 5'-phosphate, and pyridoxamine 5'-phosphate, respectively. PLP is the active cofactor form of vitamin B6 and is required for over 140 enzymatic reactions, placing PDXK at a key node of B6 salvage metabolism. Biallelic loss-of-function mutations cause an autosomal recessive neurologic disorder characterized by childhood-onset axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy predominantly affecting the lower limbs, with distal muscle weakness and atrophy, areflexia, neurogenic pain, and adult-onset optic atrophy with progressive visual impairment.

simple: PFKL is an enzyme that performs a key committed step in glycolysis, the process cells use to extract energy from sugar. Beyond energy production, it helps immune cells called macrophages decide how to use glucose during an infection. This influences the burst of reactive molecules these cells deploy to fight bacteria.
technical: PFKL is the liver-type isoform of ATP-dependent 6-phosphofructokinase, catalyzing phosphorylation of D-fructose 6-phosphate to fructose 1,6-bisphosphate, the first committing and rate-limiting step of glycolysis. It also negatively regulates the phagocyte oxidative burst during bacterial infection by controlling cellular NADPH biosynthesis and NADPH oxidase-derived reactive oxygen species. Upon macrophage activation, it drives the metabolic switch toward glycolysis, limiting glucose flux through the pentose phosphate pathway that would otherwise generate NADPH. It functions in the cytosol as part of the central glycolytic machinery; no well-established disease associations are documented here.

simple: PIGP is part of a molecular team that builds GPI anchors, the small tethers that fasten many proteins to the outer surface of cells. It helps carry out the very first step of making these anchors. When it does not work properly, children can develop a severe early-onset epilepsy with seizures that are hard to control and delays in mental and physical development.
technical: PIGP is a subunit of the GPI-N-acetylglucosaminyltransferase (GPI-GnT) complex, which catalyzes transfer of N-acetylglucosamine from UDP-N-acetylglucosamine to phosphatidylinositol, the first step of glycosylphosphatidylinositol (GPI) anchor biosynthesis at the endoplasmic reticulum. By initiating GPI assembly, it is required for the surface attachment of numerous GPI-anchored proteins. Biallelic mutations cause an autosomal recessive developmental and epileptic encephalopathy (DEE55), a severe early-onset epilepsy with refractory seizures and neurodevelopmental impairment, in which development is normal before seizure onset and cognitive and motor delays subsequently emerge.

simple: PKNOX1 is a control protein that helps switch genes on, but only when it teams up with specific partner proteins. Acting in this cooperative way lets it contribute to precise gene regulation during development. Its activity depends on the company it keeps among other regulatory proteins.
technical: PKNOX1 (PREP1) is a TALE-class homeobox transcription factor that activates transcription in the presence of partners such as PBX1A and HOXA1. As a member of the MEIS/PREP family, it functions in the nucleus by forming cooperative DNA-binding complexes with PBX and HOX proteins to confer target-site specificity and transcriptional output. Its activity is thus context-dependent, contingent on its protein partners; no well-established disease associations are documented here.

simple: POFUT2 is an enzyme that attaches a sugar called fucose onto certain proteins as they are being made, a finishing touch that helps those proteins fold and work correctly. It specifically tags proteins that carry a particular structural motif and is needed for some of them to be properly released from the cell. This kind of sugar decoration helps guide normal tissue formation during development.
technical: POFUT2 is a GDP-fucose protein O-fucosyltransferase that mediates O-linked fucosylation of proteins bearing thrombospondin type-1 repeats (TSRs) in the endoplasmic reticulum. It attaches fucose via an O-glycosidic linkage to a conserved serine or threonine within the C1-X-X-S/T-C2 consensus of TSRs, modifying members of the ADAMTS, thrombospondin, and spondin families. This modification is required for proper secretion of ADAMTS family members such as ADAMTSL1 and ADAMTS13. O-fucosylation of TSRs also contributes to restricting epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition and to correct mesoderm patterning and definitive endoderm localization; no well-established disease associations are documented here.

simple: POTED is a poorly characterized protein and little is firmly established about what it does in the body. It belongs to the POTE family, whose members are produced mainly in a small set of tissues, but its specific function is not well defined. Claims about its precise role should be treated with caution until more is known.
technical: POTED (POTE ankyrin domain family member D) is a member of the POTE family, defined by ankyrin repeat domains, but no validated molecular function is recorded in the provided grounding data. POTE family members are primarily expressed in a restricted range of tissues and some have been studied as cancer-associated antigens, yet POTED itself remains poorly characterized at the functional level. In the absence of curated function or disease annotation, specific molecular, localization, and pathway claims cannot be reliably asserted.

simple: PRDM15 is a control protein that acts as a hub in the network of switches guiding early development and decisions about what cells become. It turns on other key regulators that keep stem cells in an unspecialized, self-renewing state. Because of this, it also plays a role when ordinary cells are reprogrammed back into stem-cell-like cells.
technical: PRDM15 is a sequence-specific DNA-binding transcriptional regulator that serves as a molecular node in the transcriptional network governing embryonic development and cell fate decisions. It stimulates expression of upstream activators and repressors of the Wnt/beta-catenin and MAPK/ERK pathways, respectively, that are essential for naive pluripotency and self-renewal of embryonic stem cells, notably activating SPRY1 and RSPO1 by binding specific promoter sequences. It is involved in early embryo development and contributes to induced pluripotent stem cell reprogramming. As a PR/SET-domain zinc finger protein, it acts in the nucleus on chromatin; no well-established disease associations are documented here.

simple: PRMT2 is an enzyme that tags other proteins by adding small methyl groups to particular building blocks (arginines), a modification that changes how those proteins behave. Through this, it helps fine-tune the activity of hormone receptors and various gene-control proteins, and it can influence inflammation and cell death. These activities make it a flexible regulator of how cells respond to signals.
technical: PRMT2 is a protein arginine N-methyltransferase that methylates guanidino nitrogens of arginine residues in substrates such as STAT3, fibrillarin (FBL), and histone H4. It functions as a coactivator of androgen receptor- and estrogen receptor-mediated transactivation (the latter with estrogen) and enhances PGR, PPARG, and RARA transactivation, while also repressing E2F1 activity in an RB1-dependent manner. It may inhibit NF-kappa-B-driven transcription and promote apoptosis, and participates in C15ORF39-mediated suppression of microglial inflammatory responses by dampening NF-kappa-B signaling and pro-inflammatory cytokine production. Acting largely in the nucleus, it has also been implicated in growth regulation; no well-established disease associations are documented here.

simple: PSMG1 is a helper protein that guides the correct assembly of the proteasome, the cell's recycling machine that chews up worn-out or unwanted proteins. Working with a partner, it helps the building blocks of the proteasome snap together in the right arrangement and prevents them from joining incorrectly. Without proper assembly, cells could not dispose of damaged proteins efficiently.
technical: PSMG1 is a proteasome assembly chaperone that promotes assembly of the 20S proteasome, functioning as a heterodimer with PSMG2. The PSMG1-PSMG2 heterodimer binds the PSMA5 and PSMA7 alpha subunits, promotes assembly of the alpha subunits into the heteroheptameric alpha ring, and prevents premature dimerization of alpha rings. It thus acts transiently during proteasome biogenesis to ensure correct alpha-ring formation; no well-established disease associations are documented here.

simple: PTTG1IP is a partner protein that may help shuttle another protein, PTTG1, into the cell's nucleus where genes are controlled. By assisting this movement, it could influence processes that PTTG1 takes part in. Its function is only modestly characterized so far.
technical: PTTG1IP (pituitary tumor-transforming gene 1 protein-interacting protein) may facilitate the nuclear translocation of PTTG1 (securin). Through this interaction, it is positioned to influence PTTG1-dependent processes, although its broader molecular activities and pathway memberships remain incompletely defined in the provided data. No well-established disease associations are documented here; claims beyond its proposed role in PTTG1 nuclear import should be treated conservatively.

simple: PWP2 is part of the cellular assembly line that builds ribosomes, the tiny machines that manufacture all of a cell's proteins. It works within a large complex that helps fold, modify, and trim the raw genetic material used to make the small part of the ribosome. This makes it important for the cell's ongoing capacity to produce proteins.
technical: PWP2 (Periodic tryptophan protein 2 homolog) is a component of the small subunit (SSU) processome, the earliest precursor of the small eukaryotic ribosomal subunit. During SSU processome assembly in the nucleolus, it acts among numerous ribosome biogenesis factors, an RNA chaperone, and ribosomal proteins that associate with the nascent pre-rRNA to mediate RNA folding, modification, rearrangement, and cleavage, as well as targeted degradation of pre-rRNA by the RNA exosome. As a WD40-repeat scaffolding factor, it contributes to the orderly maturation of the 18S rRNA; no well-established disease associations are documented here.

simple: RBM11 is a protein that helps cells decide how to assemble the working instructions copied from genes, a process that lets one gene produce several slightly different products. It is active in particular tissues and appears to be involved in the maturation of nerve cells and reproductive (germ) cells. By steering these editing choices, it helps shape which protein forms a cell ultimately makes.
technical: RBM11 is a tissue-specific RNA-binding splicing factor implicated in the regulation of alternative splicing, notably during neuronal and germ cell differentiation. It can influence 5' splice site selection by binding specific exonic sequences and antagonizing the SR protein SRSF1, and it counteracts SRSF1-mediated splicing of BCL-X. Through these activities it contributes to the generation of alternative transcript isoforms in specific cell lineages. No well-established human disease association is documented for RBM11.

simple: RCAN1 acts as a brake on a cellular signaling switch called calcineurin, which helps relay messages inside cells. By holding back this switch, RCAN1 helps fine-tune signals that are important during development of the brain and nervous system. The gene sits on chromosome 21, the chromosome present in an extra copy in Down syndrome.
technical: RCAN1 (calcipressin-1) is an endogenous inhibitor of calcineurin, binding the catalytic domain of calcineurin A to suppress calcineurin-dependent transcriptional responses, including those mediated by NFAT. It has been implicated in central nervous system development. Located on chromosome 21 within the Down syndrome critical region, its dosage is of interest in trisomy 21 biology, though no specific monogenic disease is firmly assigned here in the provided data.

simple: RIPK4 is an enzyme that adds chemical tags to other proteins to pass along signals, and it is essential for skin to form and maintain itself properly. When both copies of the gene are faulty, babies can be born with severe developmental problems affecting the skin, limbs, face, and other tissues, conditions that are often life-threatening. It works hand in hand with cell programs that build a normal skin barrier.
technical: RIPK4 is a serine/threonine protein kinase required for embryonic skin development and adult skin homeostasis, acting in part by phosphorylating plakophilin-1 (PKP1) to promote keratinocyte differentiation and cell adhesion. It is a direct transcriptional target of TP63 and participates in NF-kappa-B activation. Biallelic loss-of-function mutations cause autosomal recessive disorders, including Bartsocas-Papas (lethal popliteal pterygium) syndrome and a CHAND-like syndrome, characterized by features such as pterygia, arthrogryposis, ankyloblepharon, cleft lip/palate, syndactyly, and ectodermal defects, often with early lethality.

simple: RIPPLY3 helps control which genes are switched on during development, mainly by acting as an 'off' signal for certain gene-regulating proteins. It is particularly important for the proper formation of structures in the throat and neck region of the developing embryo. By dampening the activity of a developmental regulator called TBX1, it helps guide these tissues to form correctly.
technical: RIPPLY3 is a transcriptional corepressor that negatively regulates the transcriptional activity of TBX1. It functions in the development of the pharyngeal apparatus and its derivatives. Through its modulation of TBX1, it is relevant to pharyngeal/cardiovascular developmental programs. No specific human disease is assigned to RIPPLY3 in the provided data.

simple: RRP1 is a component of the ribosome, the cell's machine for building proteins. It specifically helps during the step where the growing protein chain is extended, keeping protein production running smoothly. Because making proteins is fundamental to every cell, this role supports normal cell function and growth.
technical: RRP1 corresponds to a large ribosomal subunit acidic phosphoprotein (P1), part of the ribosomal stalk that functions in the elongation phase of translation. It facilitates the elongation step of protein synthesis on the ribosome. This is a core housekeeping role in the translational machinery. No specific human disease association is provided.

simple: RRP1B is a multitasking protein that helps build ribosomes (the cell's protein factories) and also helps switch on genes that push damaged cells toward self-destruction, a protective response after DNA damage. It has been studied in the context of cancer spread, where it can influence how likely tumors are to metastasize. Viruses such as influenza can also exploit it to help copy their own genetic material.
technical: RRP1B is a nucleolar protein that contributes to ribosome biogenesis, in part by targeting protein phosphatase PP1 to the nucleolus, and associates with chromatin where it may modulate chromatin structure. It positively regulates DNA damage-induced apoptosis as a transcriptional coactivator of proapoptotic E2F1 target genes, participates in mRNA splicing regulation, inhibits SIPA1 GTPase activity, and influences extracellular matrix gene expression. During influenza A virus infection it promotes viral mRNA transcription by facilitating binding of the viral RNA polymerase to capped mRNA. No specific Mendelian disease is assigned, though it has been studied as a metastasis-modifier locus.

simple: RSPH1 is part of the internal scaffolding that lets tiny hair-like structures called cilia, and the tails of sperm, beat and move. These moving cilia clear mucus and debris from the airways. When RSPH1 is defective, the cilia don't work properly, leading to repeated lung infections and breathing problems.
technical: RSPH1 is a component of axonemal radial spoke head complexes essential for the motility of cilia and sperm flagella. Defects cause primary ciliary dyskinesia (CILD24), characterized by abnormal motile cilia, recurrent respiratory infections, chronic inflammation, and bronchiectasis. Notably, situs inversus is typically absent in patients with RSPH1-related disease, consistent with radial spoke head defects affecting respiratory rather than nodal cilia function. The protein localizes to the ciliary/flagellar axoneme.

simple: RUNX1 is a master switch that turns on genes needed to make blood cells, and it is essential for normal blood formation. It also helps guide the development of immune cells. When the gene is altered, people can have bleeding problems from faulty platelets and a higher risk of developing leukemia (a blood cancer).
technical: RUNX1 is a runt-domain transcription factor that partners with CBFB to form core-binding factor (CBF), recognizing the consensus TGTGGT motif at enhancers and promoters to regulate hematopoiesis. It is essential for definitive hematopoiesis and governs lineage commitment in T cells (e.g., via ZBTB7B silencing), regulatory T-cell function (through FOXP3), and cytokine gene expression; multiple isoforms with differing DNA-binding and transactivation properties exist. Germline RUNX1 mutations cause familial platelet disorder with associated myeloid malignancy, an autosomal dominant condition featuring quantitative/qualitative platelet defects and predisposition to acute myelogenous leukemia. Somatic RUNX1 alterations, including the t(8;21) fusion, are recurrent in leukemia.

simple: RWDD2B is a poorly characterized protein whose precise job in the cell is not yet firmly established. It contains a recognized structural module (an RWD domain) that in other proteins is typically used to interact with partner proteins, suggesting it likely participates in protein-protein interactions. More research is needed before its specific function can be stated with confidence.
technical: RWDD2B is an RWD domain-containing protein of largely uncharacterized function; no specific molecular activity, pathway, or localization is established in the provided data. RWD domains are structurally related to the ubiquitin-conjugating (E2) enzyme fold and commonly mediate protein-protein interactions, so a scaffolding or interaction role is plausible but not confirmed for RWDD2B. The gene resides on chromosome 21. No disease association is documented; claims should remain conservative pending further study.

simple: S100B is a small protein that is especially abundant in the brain, where it is made mainly by support cells called astrocytes. It binds calcium and zinc and helps relay signals that support the growth and survival of nerve cells. Because it is released when brain cells are stressed or injured, doctors sometimes use blood levels of S100B as a marker of brain damage.
technical: S100B is a small EF-hand zinc- and calcium-binding protein, highly expressed in astrocytes and among the most abundant soluble brain proteins; it binds zinc tightly and calcium more weakly, with potassium antagonizing divalent cation binding. Functionally it acts as a neurotrophic factor promoting astrocytosis and axonal proliferation, modulates numerous target proteins in a calcium-dependent manner (e.g., activating STK38, assisting ATAD3A processing toward mitochondrial localization), and engages the receptor AGER/RAGE to influence ERK1/2 and p53/TP53 signaling, with proposed roles in myocyte apoptosis after myocardial infarction. It also contributes to sympathetic innervation of thermogenic adipose tissue. No specific Mendelian disease is provided, though S100B is widely used as a biomarker of CNS injury.

simple: SAMSN1 acts as a calming signal for immune B-cells, helping to keep their activation and multiplication in check. It also helps cells reshape their internal skeleton so they can spread, change shape, and move. Through these roles it contributes to balanced immune responses and normal cell behavior.
technical: SAMSN1 is a SAM-domain-containing adaptor protein that acts as a negative regulator of B-cell activation and dampens cell proliferation in vitro. It promotes RAC1-dependent membrane ruffling and actin cytoskeleton reorganization, regulating cell spreading and polarization, stimulates HDAC1 activity, and modulates LYN kinase activity via its tyrosine phosphorylation. These activities place it in signaling and cytoskeletal-regulatory pathways of lymphocytes. No specific Mendelian disease association is provided.

simple: SCAF4 helps make sure that when a gene's instructions are copied into RNA, the full message is produced rather than a shortened, faulty version. It does this by preventing the copying machinery from stopping or finishing too early. This quality-control role is important for normal development, and faults in the gene can lead to a neurodevelopmental disorder.
technical: SCAF4 is an SR-related, CTD-associated anti-terminator protein that prevents premature mRNA termination during transcription. Together with SCAF8, it suppresses the use of early/alternative polyadenylation sites, averting accumulation of truncated proteins; mechanistically it binds the phosphorylated CTD of RNA polymerase II (POLR2A) and associates with nascent RNA upstream of early poly(A) sites to block premature cleavage and polyadenylation, and it independently suppresses transcriptional readthrough. Loss-of-function variants cause an autosomal dominant neurodevelopmental disorder featuring mild intellectual disability, seizures, behavioral abnormalities, and skeletal/structural anomalies. The protein functions in the nucleus in association with the transcription machinery.

simple: SETD4 is an enzyme that attaches small chemical 'methyl' tags to other proteins, including the histone proteins that DNA wraps around. By placing these tags, it helps control which genes are active and influences processes such as cell growth, inflammation, antiviral defenses, and keeping certain stem cells in a resting, protected state. This makes it an important regulator of how cells behave and renew themselves.
technical: SETD4 is a SET-domain protein-lysine N-methyltransferase that methylates both histone and non-histone substrates. It mono-/dimethylates histone H3K4 to promote pro-inflammatory cytokine (IL6, TNF) transcription, trimethylates histone H4K20 to drive heterochromatin formation and maintain quiescence of stem cells (including neural stem cells), and methylates non-histone targets such as TBK1 (enhancing antiviral interferon signaling via MAVS/STING/IRF3) and XRCC5/Ku70 (driving its cytoplasmic relocalization to sequester BAX and restrain BAX-mediated apoptosis). Through these activities it regulates cell proliferation, differentiation, inflammation, and apoptosis. No specific Mendelian disease association is provided.

simple: SH3BGR is a protein whose precise biological role is not yet firmly established. Its name reflects features in its sequence (a region able to bind 'SH3' protein modules and a stretch rich in the amino acid glutamic acid), which hint at a possible role in protein interactions. The gene lies on chromosome 21, but more research is needed to define what its protein actually does.
technical: SH3BGR (SH3 domain-binding glutamic acid-rich protein) has no well-established molecular function, pathway, or localization in the provided data. Its name indicates a putative SH3-binding capacity and a glutamic acid-rich region, consistent with a possible protein-interaction role, but this is not experimentally confirmed here. The gene maps to chromosome 21 within the Down syndrome region. No specific disease association is documented; statements about its function should remain conservative.

simple: SIK1 is an enzyme that adds chemical tags to other proteins to coordinate many body processes, including how the body manages blood sugar and fat, muscle development, and the timing of the internal body clock. It can also act as a tumor suppressor, helping trigger the self-destruction of cells that have detached abnormally, which limits cancer spread. When the gene is faulty, it can cause a severe early-onset form of epilepsy with seizures and developmental delay.
technical: SIK1 is a serine/threonine kinase of the AMPK-related family that regulates gluconeogenesis, lipogenesis, cell cycle, muscle differentiation, circadian entrainment, and tumor suppression. It phosphorylates and inhibits the CREB coactivators CRTC1/2/3 (TORCs) to dampen cAMP/CREB transcription, inhibits class II HDACs (HDAC4/5) to promote MEF2 target genes in myocytes, phosphorylates SREBF1 to limit hepatic lipogenesis, contributes to p53/TP53-dependent anoikis to suppress metastasis, and acts in a sodium-sensing PPME1/PP2A/ATP1A1 pathway. Pathogenic variants cause an early-onset epileptic encephalopathy with refractory seizures, neurodevelopmental impairment, and developmental regression after normal early development.

simple: SIK1B is a predicted or poorly characterized gene, and little is firmly established about its protein product. Its name suggests it may be a close relative (paralog) of SIK1, a salt-inducible kinase, so it is presumed to resemble that family, but specific details cannot be stated with confidence. More work is needed to confirm whether and how its protein functions.
technical: SIK1B is an unresolved/predicted locus with no accession, name, function, or disease text provided, so essentially no molecular detail is firmly established. By nomenclature it appears to be a paralog of SIK1 (salt-inducible kinase 1) and is therefore predicted to resemble that AMPK-related serine/threonine kinase family, but this relationship and any catalytic activity, localization, or pathway involvement should be treated as presumed rather than confirmed. No specific function, subcellular localization, or disease association can be asserted from the available data. Any claims beyond a presumed SIK1-family resemblance would be speculative.

simple: SIM2 is a master control protein that switches genes on or off to guide development, and it is thought to be especially important in forming the brain and central nervous system. It works together with a partner protein to direct these developmental programs. The gene sits on chromosome 21, the chromosome present in an extra copy in Down syndrome.
technical: SIM2 is a basic helix-loop-helix-PAS transcription factor proposed to act as a master regulator of CNS development in cooperation with ARNT, with potentially pleiotropic effects in the tissues where it is expressed during development. It functions in the nucleus as a sequence-specific DNA-binding transcriptional regulator. Located on chromosome 21 within the Down syndrome critical region, its dosage has been of interest in trisomy 21 neurodevelopmental phenotypes. No specific monogenic disease is assigned in the provided data.

simple: SLC19A1 is a transporter that moves folate (vitamin B9) from the bloodstream into cells, which is essential for making DNA and keeping blood cells healthy. It also carries certain immune-signaling molecules into cells and is the main route by which the chemotherapy drug methotrexate enters cells. When it doesn't work, people can develop anemia and, in some cases, immune problems with recurrent infections.
technical: SLC19A1 (reduced folate carrier) is an antiporter that imports reduced folates, with high affinity for N5-methyltetrahydrofolate, by exporting intracellular organic anions down their concentration gradients. It also imports immunoreactive cyclic dinucleotides (2'-3'-cGAMP and its 3'-3' isomer), contributing to STING-pathway immune signaling, and mediates cellular uptake of the antifolate methotrexate as well as AICAR monophosphate as a counter-anion. Loss-of-function causes an autosomal recessive disorder of decreased erythrocyte folate transport (megaloblastic/hemolytic anemia with hyperhomocysteinemia and low B12 but normal serum folate, responsive to oral folate), and a separate autosomal recessive immunologic disorder of early infancy featuring recurrent infections, lymphopenia, low immunoglobulins, impaired T-cell proliferation, and poor growth. The protein localizes to the plasma membrane.

simple: SLC37A1 is a molecular shuttle in the membrane of the endoplasmic reticulum, a compartment inside our cells. It swaps a sugar-phosphate molecule (glucose-6-phosphate) for inorganic phosphate, moving each in opposite directions across the membrane. This kind of transport helps cells manage their internal supply of sugar-related building blocks.
technical: SLC37A1 is an endoplasmic reticulum membrane antiporter that exchanges cytoplasmic glucose-6-phosphate for lumenal inorganic phosphate, transporting the two in opposite directions. Its activity is independent of a lumenal glucose-6-phosphatase, distinguishing it from the classic G6PT/glucose-6-phosphatase coupling, and it is thought not to participate in homeostatic regulation of blood glucose. It belongs to the SLC37 family of sugar-phosphate exchangers localized to the ER membrane. No firmly established human disease association is documented in the provided data.

simple: SLC5A3 is a transporter in the cell's outer membrane that pulls a small sugar-like molecule called myo-inositol into the cell using sodium as the driving force. By controlling how much myo-inositol is inside, it helps cells keep the right water balance and stay the right size, which is especially important in the brain and during pregnancy. It also works alongside potassium channels to fine-tune the electrical behavior of cells.
technical: SLC5A3 is an electrogenic Na(+)-coupled symporter that actively imports myo-inositol (and scyllo-inositol) across the plasma membrane with a 2:1 Na(+)-to-sugar coupling ratio, maintaining the intracellular myo-inositol gradient that governs cell volume and osmotic balance, notably in the fetoplacental unit and central nervous system. It forms coregulatory complexes with voltage-gated K(+) channels (e.g. KCNQ1-KCNE2 in choroid plexus epithelium, KCNQ2-KCNQ3), allosterically altering channel ion selectivity, voltage dependence, and gating, while K(+) efflux reciprocally tunes Na(+)-coupled inositol influx. By supplying the myo-inositol precursor for phosphoinositide synthesis such as PI(4,5)P2, it indirectly modulates phosphoinositide-dependent ion channels and Ca(2+) signaling during osmotic stress. No firmly established human disease association is documented in the provided data.

simple: SMIM11A is a very small protein that sits within cell membranes, but its specific job has not yet been worked out. Its name simply reflects its size and membrane location rather than a known function. Little can be said with confidence about what it does in the body.
technical: SMIM11A (small integral membrane protein 11) is a short, single- or multi-pass integral membrane protein whose molecular function and pathway involvement remain uncharacterized; no curated function is available in the provided UniProt data. Although it has a reviewed accession (P58511), its localization beyond a presumed membrane association and any physiological role are not firmly established. No human disease association is documented for it in the provided data.

simple: SMIM11B appears to be a predicted or poorly characterized gene, named as a likely close relative of the small membrane protein SMIM11A. Because it lacks a confirmed protein entry and no function has been established, very little is firmly known about it. Its existence as a paralog suggests it may be a small membrane protein, but this remains a presumption rather than a fact.
technical: SMIM11B is an unresolved or predicted locus with no curated accession or function in the provided data. Its name designates it as a presumed paralog of SMIM11A (small integral membrane protein 11), so it is predicted to encode a similarly small integral membrane protein, but this relationship should be treated as inferred and no specific molecular function, localization, or pathway can be asserted. No disease association is established.

simple: SMIM34 is a small protein associated with cell membranes whose specific role has not been determined. Its name reflects its small size and membrane location rather than any known activity. At present, little can be said with confidence about its function in the body.
technical: SMIM34 (small integral membrane protein 34) is a short integral membrane protein with a reviewed accession (A8MWV9) but no curated molecular function in the provided data. Its precise subcellular localization beyond a presumed membrane association, its interacting partners, and its pathway involvement are not firmly established. No human disease association is documented for it.

simple: SOD1 is one of the body's main antioxidant defenders. It neutralizes harmful, reactive oxygen molecules (free radicals) that are produced as a byproduct of normal cell activity and that would otherwise damage cells. Mutations in SOD1 are a well-known cause of an inherited form of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), a fatal disease in which the nerve cells controlling muscles progressively die.
technical: SOD1 is the cytosolic copper-zinc superoxide dismutase that catalyzes the dismutation of toxic superoxide radicals, a frontline antioxidant defense; it also oxidizes hydrogen sulfide (H2S) to sulfate, helping detoxify H2S and limit accumulation of reactive sulfur species such as persulfides and polysulfides. Mutations in SOD1 cause a familial, often dominantly inherited form of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, a neurodegenerative disorder of upper and lower motor neurons leading to progressive fatal paralysis with sparing of sensory function, characterized pathologically by motor neuron loss, ubiquitin-positive inclusions, and aggregate deposition; SOD1 mutations account for roughly 5-10% of inherited cases. A distinct autosomal recessive presentation with early-onset loss of motor abilities, severe axial hypotonia, hyperekplexia, hypertonia, myokymia, and progressive spastic tetraparesis has also been described. The protein is predominantly cytosolic with a fraction in the mitochondrial intermembrane space.

simple: SON is a protein in the cell nucleus that helps cells correctly read and process their genetic instructions, a step called RNA splicing. It is especially important for genes involved in cell division, DNA repair, and brain development. When SON is faulty, it causes a syndrome marked by intellectual disability, developmental delay, seizures, and physical abnormalities.
technical: SON is a nuclear RNA-binding protein that acts as a splicing cofactor, promoting efficient splicing of transcripts bearing weak splice sites, including many cell-cycle and DNA-repair genes (e.g. TUBG1, KATNB1, AURKB, AKT1, RAD23A, FANCG), likely by facilitating interaction between SR proteins such as SRSF2 and RNA polymerase II. It also binds DNA at a defined consensus motif and can indirectly repress hepatitis B virus core promoter activity, and is essential for correct splicing of multiple genes critical for brain development and neuronal migration (e.g. FLNA, WDR62, PNKP). Haploinsufficiency causes ZTTK syndrome, an autosomal dominant disorder of intellectual disability, developmental delay, cortical malformations, epilepsy, visual problems, and congenital and musculoskeletal anomalies.

simple: SPATC1L is a protein whose specific job has not been clearly established. Its name relates it to speriolin, a protein found in sperm cells, so it may play a role in reproductive cells, but this is not confirmed. Little firm information is available about what it does.
technical: SPATC1L (speriolin-like protein) has no curated molecular function in the provided data, though its naming relates it to the speriolin (SPATC1) family associated with the spermatid/sperm cytoskeleton and centrosome, suggesting a possible role in germ cells that remains to be firmly established. Its precise localization, interactions, and pathway involvement are not documented here. No human disease association is established in the provided data.

simple: SUMO3 is a small tag that cells attach to other proteins to change how those proteins behave, a process called SUMOylation. Rather than marking proteins for destruction, this tag adjusts activities such as moving things in and out of the nucleus, copying and repairing DNA, and cell division. It is a versatile regulator that helps keep many cellular processes running correctly.
technical: SUMO3 is a ubiquitin-like modifier covalently conjugated to substrate lysines, as a monomer or lysine-linked polymer, that regulates processes including nuclear transport, DNA replication and repair, mitosis, and signal transduction; unlike ubiquitin it is generally not a degradation signal and may antagonize ubiquitin-mediated degradation. Conjugation requires activation by the SAE1-SAE2 (E1) complex, transfer via the E2 enzyme UBE2I (Ubc9), and can be stimulated by E3 ligases such as PIAS1-4, RANBP2, or CBX4. It contributes to regulating the sumoylation status of substrates such as the senataxin SETX. No firmly established human disease association is documented in the provided data.

simple: SYNJ1 is an enzyme that removes phosphate groups from membrane lipids called phosphoinositides, helping to recycle the small sacs (vesicles) that nerve cells use to communicate. By controlling these lipids, it is essential for the rapid recovery of synaptic vesicles after signals are sent between neurons. Mutations in SYNJ1 cause an early-onset form of Parkinson's disease and a severe childhood epilepsy.
technical: SYNJ1 (synaptojanin-1) is a dual-function phosphoinositide phosphatase: its 5-phosphatase domain removes the 5-phosphate from substrates such as PtdIns(4,5)P2, PtdIns(3,4,5)P3, Ins(1,4,5)P3, and Ins(1,3,4,5)P4, while its SAC domain hydrolyzes 3- and 4-position phosphates from PI(3)P, PI(4)P, and PI(3,5)P2. It functions in synaptic vesicle recycling, including neck formation during ultrafast endocytosis and clathrin uncoating of vesicles budding from synaptic endosomes, and regulates actin rearrangement by dephosphorylating PIP2 on actin-regulatory proteins. Mutations cause autosomal recessive early-onset parkinsonism (PARK20), with features such as seizures, cognitive decline, abnormal eye movements, and dystonia, and an autosomal recessive developmental and epileptic encephalopathy (DEE53) with refractory seizures and neurodevelopmental regression after normal early development.

simple: TCP10L is a protein thought to help control which genes are switched on or off. In laboratory studies it can dampen gene activity and acts to suppress the growth of liver cancer cells, suggesting a protective, tumor-restraining role. Its full set of functions in the body is not yet well defined.
technical: TCP10L (T-complex protein 10A homolog 1) is implicated in transcriptional regulation, displaying transcription inhibition activity in vitro and behaving as a tumor suppressor in hepatocellular carcinoma cells. Its precise DNA/partner targets and detailed mechanism are not fully defined in the provided data, and its localization is presumed nuclear consistent with a transcriptional role. No inherited human disease association is documented here beyond its reported tumor-suppressive behavior in HCC.

simple: TFF1 is a small secreted protein that helps protect the lining of the stomach and gut. It stabilizes the protective mucus layer that shields these tissues from acid and other irritants. It may also help prevent certain kidney stone crystals from growing in urine.
technical: TFF1 (trefoil factor 1) is a secreted trefoil-domain peptide that stabilizes the mucous gel overlying the gastrointestinal mucosa, providing a physical barrier against noxious agents and supporting mucosal protection. It may also inhibit the growth of calcium oxalate crystals in urine. It is characteristically expressed in gastric mucosa and acts in the extracellular/luminal compartment. No firmly established inherited disease association is documented in the provided data.

simple: TFF2 is a small secreted protein that helps protect the stomach. It slows the movement of the gut and reduces stomach acid secretion, and it strengthens the protective mucus barrier lining the digestive tract. Together these actions help guard the stomach lining from damage.
technical: TFF2 (trefoil factor 2) is a secreted trefoil-domain peptide that inhibits gastrointestinal motility and gastric acid secretion and may act as a structural component of gastric mucus, plausibly by stabilizing mucin glycoproteins through interactions with their carbohydrate side chains. It functions in the gastric/intestinal lumen and mucus gel layer, contributing to mucosal protection. No firmly established human disease association is documented in the provided data.

simple: TFF3 is a small secreted protein that helps maintain and repair the lining of the intestine. It encourages the cells of this lining to move and close over wounds, speeding up healing. This makes it important for keeping the gut barrier intact.
technical: TFF3 (trefoil factor 3, intestinal trefoil factor) is a secreted trefoil-domain peptide involved in maintenance and repair of the intestinal mucosa, acting as a motogen that promotes epithelial cell migration during restitution and wound healing. It functions in the extracellular/luminal compartment within the protective mucus layer. No firmly established human disease association is documented in the provided data.

simple: TIAM1 is a switch-flipping protein that turns on a family of molecular switches (Rho-like proteins) controlling the cell's internal scaffolding. By doing so, it links signals from outside the cell to changes in cell shape, how cells stick together, and how they move. When TIAM1 is faulty, it causes a recessive disorder with developmental delay, intellectual disability, and seizures.
technical: TIAM1 is a guanine nucleotide exchange factor (GEF) that activates Rho-family GTPases, principally RAC1 and CDC42 (and to a lesser extent RHOA), coupling extracellular signals to actin cytoskeletal dynamics and downstream signaling that govern cell adhesion and migration. It operates at the interface of receptor signaling and cytoskeletal remodeling. Biallelic loss-of-function variants cause an autosomal recessive neurodevelopmental disorder with global developmental delay, intellectual disability, speech delay, and seizures, sometimes with axial hypotonia, peripheral hypertonia, hypothyroidism, dysmorphic features, or brain imaging abnormalities.

simple: TMEM50B is a small protein embedded in cell membranes whose specific role has not been clearly determined. Its name simply notes that it spans a membrane. Little is firmly known about what it does in the body.
technical: TMEM50B (transmembrane protein 50B) is a multi-pass membrane protein with no curated molecular function in the provided data; it is presumed to reside in intracellular membranes but its precise localization, interactions, and pathway role are not firmly established. No human disease association is documented for it in the provided data.

simple: TMPRSS15, also called enteropeptidase, is an enzyme in the small intestine that kick-starts digestion. It activates trypsinogen, turning it into trypsin, which then switches on a cascade of other digestive enzymes that break down food. Without working enteropeptidase, the body cannot properly digest proteins, causing a serious malabsorption disorder with diarrhea and failure to thrive in infants.
technical: TMPRSS15 (enteropeptidase/enterokinase) is an intestinal brush-border serine protease that initiates the activation of pancreatic proteolytic zymogens by converting trypsinogen to trypsin, which in turn activates chymotrypsinogen, procarboxypeptidases, and proelastases. It localizes to the apical membrane of duodenal enterocytes and is the gatekeeper of intraluminal protein digestion. Loss-of-function leads to enteropeptidase deficiency, a life-threatening intestinal malabsorption disorder characterized by diarrhea and failure to thrive.

simple: TMPRSS2 is an enzyme anchored to the surface of cells that cuts other proteins to switch them on, with normal roles in the prostate. It became widely known because viruses, including the coronaviruses that cause SARS and COVID-19, hijack it to help them enter human cells. It also activates proteins linked to the spread of prostate cancer.
technical: TMPRSS2 is a plasma membrane-anchored type II transmembrane serine protease that cleaves substrates at arginine residues and participates in prostate proteolytic cascades; androgen-induced TMPRSS2 activates substrates such as pro-HGF, the protease-activated receptor F2RL1, and matriptase/ST14, promoting extracellular matrix disruption and prostate cancer metastasis. It is a key host factor for respiratory virus entry, priming coronavirus spike glycoproteins (SARS-CoV, SARS-CoV-2 cleaved between S2 and S2', HCoV-229E, HCoV-EMC) and cleaving ACE2 to promote SARS-CoV/SARS-CoV-2 uptake and syncytia formation, as well as activating fusion glycoproteins of Sendai virus, HMPV, and parainfluenza viruses and the hemagglutinin of influenza A (H1N1, H3N2, H7N9). It can also serve as a receptor for HCoV-HKU1, acting synergistically with disialoside glycans. No inherited human disease association is documented in the provided data.

simple: TMPRSS3 is a protein that helps cut and activate other proteins, and it plays an important role in hearing. It helps keep the delicate sound-sensing cells of the inner ear alive and working, especially around the time hearing begins. When this protein doesn't work properly, it can cause a form of inherited hearing loss.
technical: TMPRSS3 is a probable type II transmembrane serine protease implicated in cochlear and saccular hair cell survival and activation at the onset of hearing, and it can activate the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) in vitro. It is membrane-anchored in inner ear sensory epithelia. Loss-of-function mutations cause non-syndromic sensorineural hearing loss, reflecting damage to the neural receptors of the inner ear.

simple: TPTE is a poorly understood protein that is thought to help relay signals inside cells. Its exact job is not firmly established, but it appears to participate in cellular signaling. Little is known about its importance to health or disease.
technical: TPTE is a putative protein tyrosine phosphatase that has been proposed to function in signal transduction, though its specific substrates and physiological role remain poorly characterized. It is related to the PTEN family of phosphatases by sequence. No well-established disease associations have been documented for this protein.

simple: TRAPPC10 is part of a cellular delivery system that helps move and dock small membrane packages within the cell, particularly at a sorting station called the Golgi. This traffic-control role is important for cells to function normally. When the protein is faulty, it can lead to a severe inherited disorder with developmental delay, small head size, and intellectual disability.
technical: TRAPPC10 is a specific subunit of the TRAPP II complex, a conserved multisubunit vesicle-tethering complex that acts as a membrane tether in late Golgi trafficking. It localizes to the Golgi and supports intracellular vesicular transport. Biallelic loss-of-function variants cause an autosomal recessive neurodevelopmental disorder featuring global developmental delay, short stature, severe intellectual disability, microcephaly, impaired speech, and behavioral abnormalities including autistic features.

simple: TRPM2 is a channel that sits in cell membranes and lets charged particles such as calcium, sodium, and zinc flow into the cell. It opens in response to signals like oxidative stress, warmth, and a molecule called ADP-ribose, allowing the cell to respond to its environment. Through these calcium and zinc signals it influences processes such as immune defense, body temperature regulation, insulin release, and, under harmful stress, cell death.
