technical: TRPM2 is a nonselective, voltage-independent cation channel that conducts Na+ and Ca2+ to raise cytoplasmic Ca2+, and it is gated by intracellular ADP-ribose (binding its cytoplasmic Nudix domain), Ca2+, warm temperature, and oxidative stress. It also mediates release of lysosomal Zn2+ in response to reactive oxygen species and contributes to Ca2+ release from intracellular stores. Functionally it participates in insulin secretion, body temperature homeostasis, innate immunity (cytokine secretion, phagocytosis, dendritic cell function), actin/filopodia remodeling, and confers susceptibility to oxidative-stress-induced cell death; a truncated isoform lacks channel activity and negatively regulates the full-length channel.

simple: TSPEAR helps guide the formation of teeth and hair follicles by tuning an important developmental signaling system, and it may also contribute to how the hearing system develops. When it is defective, people can have problems with structures that grow from the skin, such as missing or cone-shaped teeth, sparse hair, and nail or sweat-gland abnormalities. Some individuals also have inherited hearing loss.
technical: TSPEAR is a thrombospondin-type laminin G domain and EAR-repeat-containing protein that regulates tooth and hair follicle morphogenesis through the Notch signaling pathway and may contribute to auditory system development. Biallelic variants cause non-syndromic sensorineural hearing loss with prelingual onset, an autosomal recessive ectodermal dysplasia (ECTD14) featuring scalp hypotrichosis, hypodontia, conical teeth, and facial dysmorphism, and autosomal recessive selective tooth agenesis (STHAG10). These phenotypes reflect its role in ectodermal appendage development.

simple: TTC3 is a quality-control protein that tags certain other proteins for disposal, helping the cell keep their levels in check. In particular, it helps switch off a major growth-signaling protein called Akt after it has done its job, and it influences how nerve cells mature. Through these activities it helps control cell growth and development.
technical: TTC3 is an E3 ubiquitin-protein ligase that catalyzes 'Lys-48'-linked polyubiquitination and mediates nuclear degradation of phosphorylated Akt (AKT1/2/3), acting as a terminal regulator of Akt signaling following its activation. It also ubiquitinates SMURF2 to promote TGFB1-induced epithelial-mesenchymal transition and myofibroblast differentiation, and it regulates neuronal differentiation via a RHOA-CIT-ROCK cascade affecting actin remodeling and Golgi organization. It inhibits cell proliferation. No firmly established disease associations are noted in the provided data.

simple: U2AF1 helps cells read their genes correctly by marking exactly where pieces of genetic message should be joined together, a process called splicing. By recognizing the right join sites, it ensures proteins are made accurately. Mutations in this protein are linked to bone marrow disorders that disrupt blood cell production and can sometimes progress to leukemia.
technical: U2AF1 is the 35 kDa subunit of the U2 small nuclear ribonucleoprotein auxiliary factor, essential for constitutive and enhancer-dependent pre-mRNA splicing through protein-protein and protein-RNA interactions that ensure accurate 3'-splice site selection. It recruits U2 snRNP to the branch point and bridges U2AF2 to enhancer-bound complexes. Recurrent somatic mutations cause myelodysplastic syndromes, a group of clonal hematopoietic disorders with dysplasia and peripheral cytopenias that can progress to acute myeloid leukemia.

simple: U2AF1L5 is a predicted or poorly characterized gene, and very little is firmly established about its protein product. Its name suggests it may resemble the splicing factor U2AF1, but this is not confirmed and no specific function can be stated with confidence.
technical: U2AF1L5 is an unresolved or predicted locus with no assigned reviewed accession and no curated functional annotation, so essentially nothing is firmly established about its protein. Its symbol implies a presumed relationship to the U2AF1 splicing-factor family, but any role in 3'-splice-site recognition is speculative and unverified. No subcellular localization, pathway, or disease association can be reliably assigned from the available data.

simple: UBASH3A is a regulatory protein that helps keep certain cell-surface receptors active by interfering with the machinery that would normally break them down. This affects how immune cells, such as T cells, and growth-signaling receptors respond to stimulation. It is thought to fine-tune immune and signaling responses rather than carry out a strong enzyme activity of its own.
technical: UBASH3A is a ubiquitin-associated and SH3 domain-containing protein that interferes with CBL-mediated downregulation and degradation of receptor-type tyrosine kinases, promoting cell-surface accumulation of activated receptors such as the T-cell receptor, EGFR, and PDGFRB. It exhibits negligible protein tyrosine phosphatase activity at neutral pH and may act as a dominant-negative regulator of UBASH3B-dependent dephosphorylation. Via its SH3 domain it may sequester dynamin and inhibit dynamin-dependent endocytic pathways. No specific disease association is documented in the provided data.

simple: UBE2G2 is part of the cell's labeling system that marks unwanted or damaged proteins for destruction. It attaches a small tag called ubiquitin onto target proteins so they can be cleared away. This helps the cell dispose of faulty proteins, including some made in the endoplasmic reticulum, and regulate cholesterol-related enzymes.
technical: UBE2G2 is an E2 ubiquitin-conjugating enzyme that accepts ubiquitin from the E1 complex and catalyzes its covalent attachment to substrates, building 'Lys-48'-linked polyubiquitin chains in vitro. It functions in endoplasmic reticulum-associated degradation (ERAD) and is required for sterol-induced ubiquitination of HMG-CoA reductase and its subsequent proteasomal degradation. No well-established disease association is noted in the provided data.

simple: UMODL1 is a uromodulin-like protein whose precise function is not well established. Based on its name and family, it resembles uromodulin, a protein associated with the urinary tract, but its specific role is not firmly defined. Little is confirmed about its importance to health or disease.
technical: UMODL1 (uromodulin-like 1) is a protein named for its similarity to uromodulin, but it lacks detailed curated functional annotation, so its molecular function, localization, and pathway involvement remain poorly defined. Any inferred role should be treated as conservative and based primarily on family resemblance rather than direct evidence. No well-established disease association can be reliably assigned from the available data.

simple: URB1 is a protein associated with the nucleolus, the part of the cell nucleus where ribosomes are assembled. Based on its name, it is thought to help build ribosomes, the machines that make proteins. Its detailed function is not extensively characterized, and no firm disease links are established here.
technical: URB1 is a nucleolar pre-ribosomal-associated protein, implicated by its annotation in ribosome biogenesis, likely participating in maturation of pre-ribosomal particles within the nucleolus. Detailed mechanistic and functional data are limited in the provided source, so specific claims should remain conservative. No well-established disease association is documented in the available data.

simple: USP16 is an enzyme that removes small ubiquitin tags from other proteins, reversing a kind of cellular labeling. One of its key jobs is to strip a tag from a DNA-packaging protein (histone H2A), which switches genes back on and helps chromosomes separate correctly when cells divide. It also helps ribosomes mature and stabilizes certain other proteins.
technical: USP16 is a ubiquitin carboxyl-terminal hydrolase (deubiquitinase) that specifically removes ubiquitin from 'Lys-120' of histone H2A (H2AK119Ub), relieving Polycomb-type transcriptional repression and acting as a coactivator; this deubiquitination is a prerequisite for histone H3 Ser-10 phosphorylation and proper chromosome segregation at mitotic entry. It prefers nucleosomal substrates, regulates Hox gene expression, and its activity is enhanced by AURKB phosphorylation in resting lymphocytes. It also deubiquitinates non-histone substrates including RPS27A (promoting 40S ribosomal subunit maturation) and tektins (stabilizing them). No well-established disease association is noted in the provided data.

simple: USP25 is an enzyme that removes ubiquitin tags from proteins, helping control processes such as inflammation and immune defense. By stabilizing or protecting specific partner proteins, it fine-tunes pathways involved in immunity, antioxidant defense, and antiviral responses. When it is faulty, it has been linked to an inherited form of epilepsy.
technical: USP25 is a deubiquitinating enzyme that hydrolyzes ubiquitin from substrates to regulate inflammation and immune responses: it stabilizes tankyrases TNKS1/2 to modulate Wnt/beta-catenin signaling, deubiquitinates KEAP1 to dampen the NRF2 antioxidant program, deubiquitinates ERLIN1/2 to support RNA virus-induced innate signaling, and removes 'Lys-63'-linked ubiquitin from TRAF5/TRAF6 to negatively regulate IL-17 signaling. A muscle-specific isoform (USP25m) may contribute to muscular differentiation. Variants are associated with an autosomal dominant form of idiopathic generalized epilepsy (EIG19), with variable seizure types arising in the first two decades of life.

simple: VPS26C is part of a cellular recycling system that retrieves proteins from the cell's internal sorting compartments and sends them back to the surface instead of letting them be degraded. This recycling keeps many surface proteins available for tasks like cell movement, adhesion, and nutrient uptake. It works as part of larger complexes that capture and reuse specific cargo proteins.
technical: VPS26C is a component of the retriever complex, a heterotrimeric assembly related to the retromer cargo-selective complex, and of the larger commander complex (retriever plus CCC subcomplex) that drives retromer-independent endosomal recycling of transmembrane cargos. Recruited to endosomal membranes with the CCC and WASH complexes, it couples to SNX17 to recycle NxxY-motif cargos such as integrin alpha-5/beta-1, supporting cell migration, adhesion, nutrient supply, and signaling. It has also been implicated in microbial trafficking, mediating human papillomavirus exit to the cell surface. No well-established disease association is noted in the provided data.

simple: WDR4 is a helper protein that, together with a partner enzyme, adds a small chemical mark to RNA molecules, including the transfer RNAs that help build proteins. This mark stabilizes RNA and supports accurate protein production, and WDR4 also helps protect the genome during DNA copying. When it is defective, it can cause severe inherited disorders affecting brain growth, the kidneys, and overall development.
technical: WDR4 is the non-catalytic, scaffolding subunit of the METTL1-WDR4 methyltransferase complex that installs N7-methylguanine (m7G), notably at position 46 (m7G46) of tRNAs bearing a 5'-RAGGU-3' variable-loop motif, stabilizing tRNA tertiary structure and protecting against decay; it also supports m7G modification of subsets of mRNAs and miRNAs (e.g., let-7). Independently of METTL1, it localizes to DNA replication sites and regulates FEN1 endonuclease activity to maintain genome stability. Biallelic variants cause Galloway-Mowat syndrome 6 (GAMOS6) with microcephaly, nephrotic syndrome, brain malformations, and developmental delay, as well as a related autosomal recessive disorder with growth retardation, microcephaly, seizures, and brain anomalies.

simple: YBEY is an enzyme that cuts RNA and helps complete the assembly of ribosomal RNA, a key building block of the ribosomes that make proteins. By trimming RNA into its mature form, it supports proper protein production in the cell. Its broader role in human health is not well characterized here.
technical: YBEY is a single-strand-specific metallo-endoribonuclease involved in ribosomal RNA maturation. It uses a divalent metal-dependent catalytic mechanism to process rRNA precursors, contributing to ribosome assembly. No well-established disease association is documented in the provided data.

simple: ZBTB21 is a protein that helps control which genes are switched on or off, acting mainly to turn genes down. By repressing gene activity, it contributes to regulating cellular programs. Its specific biological roles and any links to disease are not well characterized here.
technical: ZBTB21 is a zinc finger and BTB domain-containing protein that functions as a transcriptional repressor, with the BTB domain typically mediating protein interactions and the zinc fingers conferring DNA binding. It is expected to localize to the nucleus consistent with its role in transcriptional regulation. No well-established disease association is documented in the provided data.
